coredata.dc:description: The optoelectronic properties and the high biocompatibility of carbon quantum dots have made them one of the most novel and studied nanomaterials in recent years. Several application areas have used them as an efficient, economical, and environmentally sustainable tool. This review article is divided into five sections. The first unit presents the classification, description, and distinction of different types of quantum materials, emphasizing significant structural differences. The second section delves into carbon quantum dots, detailing the synthesis routes and presenting the mechanisms and variables considered in each case. The third section explains the chemical structure, stability, and related optical behaviors in detail. The fourth section highlights the most prominent applications of CQDs, such as the development of sensors, the photocatalysis of pollutants, imaging, nanomedicine, the oil and gas industry, cancer treatment, plant growth promoter and antibacterial applications. In the Fifth section, challenges and perspectives are discussed. To conduct a systematic analysis of the information, a large volume of data from the literature related to each section is processed, and a meta-analysis is developed for a more exhaustive processing of the information.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The optoelectronic properties and the high biocompatibility of carbon quantum dots have made them one of the most novel and studied nanomaterials in recent years. Several application areas have used them as an efficient, economical, and environmentally sustainable tool. This review article is divided into five sections. The first unit presents the classification, description, and distinction of different types of quantum materials, emphasizing significant structural differences. The second section delves into carbon quantum dots, detailing the synthesis routes and presenting the mechanisms and variables considered in each case. The third section explains the chemical structure, stability, and related optical behaviors in detail. The fourth section highlights the most prominent applications of CQDs, such as the development of sensors, the photocatalysis of pollutants, imaging, nanomedicine, the oil and gas industry, cancer treatment, plant growth promoter and antibacterial applications. In the Fifth section, challenges and perspectives are discussed. To conduct a systematic analysis of the information, a large volume of data from the literature related to each section is processed, and a meta-analysis is developed for a more exhaustive processing of the information.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nomenclature

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology is essential in many industrial applications, transforming human life by providing new materials, tools, and processes [1]. Nanomaterials possess desirable properties such as improved strength [2], conductivity [3], and thermal stability [4], which are sought by industrial sectors to improve the performance and durability of their products and technologies [5]. An exciting group of nanomaterials that has experienced a significant academic boom in recent years is that of “quantum materials." This group includes quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs).

QDs with sizes smaller than 10 nm [6] are a type of nanomaterial that has garnered significant attention from researchers due to their exceptional optical and electronic properties [7–10]. Their quantum confinement allows them to have electronic, catalytic, and optical tuning. When QDs are stimulated by light, they emit fluorescence of a particular color dependent on their size [11]. As the size of the quantum dots decreases, the bandgap increases, leading to higher energy emissions [12,13]. Several strategies have been developed to obtain QDs, including colloidal [14], chemical vapor [15], and electrochemical synthesis [16], among others [17–19]. Each method provides a way to regulate QD size, shape, and surface characteristics, influencing their effectiveness in specific applications [20]. The areas of application for quantum dots are wide. Although they initially emerged for lighting due to their ability to emit at different wavelengths and for improving the efficiency of solar cells, other important areas, such as bioimaging, sensing, and electronics, have taken advantage of their potential for visualizing cellular structures, detecting various chemicals, and developing advanced transistors, respectively [21]. However, concerns have been raised about the potential toxicity of certain substances used in quantum dot syntheses, such as cadmium and selenium, increasing manufacturing costs at a large scale [22].

Additionally, producing and disposing of QDs that contain heavy metals can contribute to environmental pollution [23]. This raises questions about their long-term impact as they are sensitive to environmental factors [24] and can deteriorate over time [25]. As a result, carbon-based structures have received considerable attention as a safer alternative [26]. The CDs include graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [27], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [28], carbon nanodots (CNDs) [29], and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [30]. Currently, different carbon-rich precursors [31] like citric acid [32], glucose [33], or waste materials like cane bagasse [34], orange peel [35], coconut husk [36] and many others have been used as precursors to obtain CQDs. These systems also show quantum confinement effects because of their small size and can be synthesized using a variety of techniques, including pyrolysis [37], hydrothermal [38], and microwave-assisted techniques [39]. These methods enable scientists to control the CQDs' dimensions, form, and surface characteristics [40].

Additionally, their surface functionalization improves their solubility, stability, and environment compatibility [41–43]. Because CQDs are low in toxicity and have good biocompatibility, they are particularly beneficial for biological, medical, and environmentally sensitive applications [44]. In the literature, several applications are documented for CQDs, for example, as tracers, CQDs excellent fluorescence is used to monitor biological and environmental processes [45]. CQDs assist with high sensitivity and selectivity in chemical sensing and biosensing, detecting ions, biomolecules, and molecules [46]. Their biocompatibility and luminescence make them ideal for bioimaging applications, giving high-resolution cellular and tissue imaging [47]. In nanomedicine, CQDs are used for targeted drug delivery, enhancing therapeutic efficacy and reducing side effects [48]. CQDs also show applications in photocatalysis and electrocatalysis, enhancing the efficiency of energy conversion processes [49,50]. CQDs are incorporated into membranes in membrane technology to improve their permeability, selectivity, and anti-fouling properties, making them valuable for water purification and separation [51]. Even the oil and gas industry has made important advances using CQDs to improve the rheological performance of drilling fluids, inhibition/remediation of formation damage, as inter-well tracers [52–54], and other applications with promising results and successful field implementations.

This document describes each type of "quantum material" to clarify their characteristics and avoid indiscriminate use. It emphasizes the characteristics, synthesis methodologies, and applications of CQDs, aiming to highlight the advances and developments made from 2020 to 2024. To achieve this, the first section of the document classifies quantum materials into two major groups: those synthesized primarily with heavy metals (QDs) and those based on carbon (CDs). Each subcategory is discussed in detail, and the fundamental structural differences are established. The second section focuses on carbon quantum dots, a special type of CD obtained through different routes with either a bottom-up or top-down approach. Each route is described in detail, mentioning the operating variables and the basis for synthesizing CQDs. A meta-analysis is conducted to show how certain operating variables influence the efficiency of the obtained material and specific characteristics. The third section describes the structural, optical, and stability characteristics that distinguish CQDs, including the type of electronic transitions that produce optical phenomena such as photoluminescence, fluorescence, and phosphorescence. It details how the functionalization of the structure with different heteroatoms acts as a surface modifier for the nanomaterial. The section also covers how factors such as UV light, temperature, salts, and pH levels affect the stability of CQDs, particularly regarding thermal, pressure, photostability, and colloidal stability. Finally, the fourth section focuses on the most prominent applications of this type of nanomaterial and the latest advances in each area. It summarizes how CQDs have been used to detect various analytes with high specificity, achieving very low detection limits, and describes the interaction mechanisms between the analyte and the CQDs. Then, it discusses the photocatalysis of different contaminants and how their degradation is more efficiently achieved using CQDs. In bioimaging, the most important developments for obtaining highly efficient and increasingly specific cellular images are presented. In biosensing, the most detailed studies on labeling important biomolecules such as DNA and proteins are analyzed. In the nanomedicine section, more specific applications where CQDs were used in the treatment of target cells are described, especially tumor cells that can be selectively treated with CQDs with ligands on their surface, allowing for specific action. Other applications including, the oil and gas industry, cancer treatment, plant growth promoter and antibacterial applications were also discussed in this section. To process this information consciously and systematically, each section was systematically analyzed to generate a meta-analysis that provided never-before-reported information and descriptions. This document presents the benefits of comprehensive and systematic reviews to conduct a more detailed analysis and allow optimized information processing regarding the applications of CQDs. The novelty is marked by its comprehensive and interdisciplinary scope. Among the most recent reviews related to CDs is the work by Wang et al. [55], which provides a general overview of advancements in CD synthesis, with particular attention to synthesis techniques and operating conditions aimed at achieving specific properties. However, this work offers limited insight into the practical applications of CDs across various industries, including Oil and Gas, thereby creating a disconnect between fundamental research and real-world implementation. Moreover, the authors restrict their focus to a narrow set of CD types—namely graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs).

In contrast, the present review adopts a broader and more robust perspective, compiling detailed information and focused information on at least seven distinct structural types of carbon-based dots. This distinction is crucial, given that while all these materials are light-emitting nanostructures, CDs are characterized by their carbon-based composition, environmental friendliness, and high biocompatibility. In comparison, conventional quantum dots (QDs), typically composed of inorganic semiconductors, allow for more precise control over optical properties but raise greater concerns regarding toxicity, which can limit their scalability and applicability in certain environments.

Huang and Ren [56], offer one of the most recent and comprehensive reviews focused on large-scale synthesis of CDs, categorizing techniques into liquid, gaseous, solid, and emerging methods based on efficiency and scalability. Nonetheless, their discussion of the physicochemical and optoelectronic properties relevant to practical applications remains limited. Notably, the volume of literature reviewed by Huang and Ren represents only about 50 % of the sources analyzed in the present work.

Several other recent reviews have concentrated on niche applications of CDs, including food industry [57], battery design [58], bioimaging [59], and antiviral activity [60]. In contrast, this review delivers an integrative and exhaustive assessment, encompassing a broad definition of CDs, synthesis and functionalization strategies, characterization techniques, and a wide range of applications—particularly in the energy sector. Furthermore, this work distinguishes itself not only by the number of studies reviewed (over 500 peer-reviewed papers published in the last five years), but also by its selection of representative contributions from each field. To enhance clarity and accessibility for readers, the results are synthesized and presented through visual aids such as pie charts.

This review provides a comprehensive and systematic analysis of the existing literature on CQDs applications, employing a structured methodology. The first step involved a thorough search across multiple academic databases, including Scopus, Web of Science, and Google Scholar. The search focused on four distinct components: (1) various terminologies used for CQDs, such as “Carbon Quantum Dots,” “CQD,” and related expressions; (2) a range of applications, including “sensors,” “bioimaging,” “nanomedicine,” and others; (3) applications specific to the petroleum industry, such as “drilling” and “enhanced oil recovery”; and (4) studies published between 2020 and 2024 to ensure the inclusion of recent advancements. The second step involved selecting studies based on their relevance to the topic, the quality of experimental data, and their contributions to understanding CQDs applications, stability, and environmental impact. Both experimental and review articles were included to ensure a well-rounded overview of the field. Next, relevant data were extracted from the selected studies, including synthesis methods, CQD properties, and performance metrics across various applications. Comparative analyses were conducted to identify trends, challenges, and innovations in CQD design and utilization. Finally, each study was critically evaluated to assess the strengths and limitations of different approaches. Particular attention was given to the practical implications of the findings, the relevance of state-of-the-art technologies, and potential future research directions. By synthesizing the results from individual studies, this review offers a holistic understanding of CQD applications across diverse fields, highlighting recent advancements, existing gaps, and opportunities for future exploration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differences between quantum dots and carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Despite the wide variety of uses and applications of nanomaterials with fluorescent characteristics, it is sometimes difficult to differentiate between the great variety of quantum materials reported in the literature. This section aims to clarify important definitions and differences between the available materials. Fig. 1 shows the classification of quantum materials described in the document.

As is mentioned by the Nobel prize in chemistry [61], Quantum dots constitute a new class of materials that is neither molecular nor bulk. They have the same structure and atomic composition as bulk materials, but their properties can be tuned using a single parameter: the particle's size [62]. Also known as semiconductor nanocrystals, possess optical and electronic properties different from larger particles through quantum mechanical effects [63]. Essentially, they are made of semiconductor materials, with the most common being cadmium selenide (CdSe), cadmium sulfide (CdS), cadmium telluride (CdTe), indium phosphide (InP), indium arsenide (InAs), silicon (Si), among others [64–66]. QDs have the property of tightly confining electrons or electron holes. This confinement is similar to a three-dimensional particle-in-a-box model [67], in which the allowed electronic transitions between the different discrete energy levels in the box match the absorption and emission patterns of the material [68]. Therefore, QDs are called artificial atoms, as their bound and discrete electronic states are like those of naturally occurring atoms and molecules. Koley et al. [69] have shown how coupling two or more QDs can create an artificial molecule that exhibits hybridization at room temperature. The unique physical properties of quantum dots, such as high photoluminescence quantum yield, tunable emission wavelengths, and resistance to the optical oscillator at low concentrations, made them very attractive for their use in optoelectronic devices, such as light emitters, light diodes, solar cells, lasers and bio-imaging. According to their nanostructure, quantum dots can be classified into three essential groups: core-type quantum dots, core-shell-type quantum dots, and alloyed quantum dots.

Core-type quantum dots are the "simplest" as they are single-component nanodots with a uniform internal composition throughout the structure. They are generally composed of chalcogenides (a combination of a group 16 anion and an electropositive metal). The most common metals in the structure are Cd, Se, and Zn [70–72], and the most common core type QDs of this kind are CdTe-QD, Se-QD, and ZnS-QD [73,74]. However, other metals like Silicon, Copper, Indium, and Gallium have also been used to obtain Ct-QDs. According to the authors, the electrical and photoluminescence properties can be adjusted by simply changing the crystallite size [75].

(CSQDs), also known as core-shell semiconductor nanocrystals (CSSNCs), consist of a semiconductor material coated with a shell of another semiconductor material. The core and the shell are typically made from materials commonly used in Ct-QDs, with combinations such as CdS/ZnS, CdSe/ZnS, CdSe/CdS, and InAs/CdSe, among others [76]. The core-shell quantum dot strategy emerged as an efficient alternative to improve the fluorescence quantum yield. The luminescent properties of quantum dots arise from the recombination of electron-hole pairs (a process known as exciton decay) through radiative processes, releasing energy in the form of light (photons). However, this decay can also occur through non-radiative pathways, where power is dissipated as heat or through other interactions with the material's environment, resulting in reduced fluorescence quantum yield [77]. Thus, coating a semiconductor material with another having a higher band gap allows for the passivation of non-radiative recombination sites, enhancing optical properties with quantum yields up to 50 % higher [78]. This also results in quantum dots that are much more robust and resistant to processing and application conditions.

Alloyed quantum dots are nanoparticles composed of at least two different semiconductor materials. These are formed by combining semiconductors with varying band gap energies, resulting in structures with unique optoelectronic properties distinct from the original materials. The distribution of these properties within the quantum dot can be either homogeneous or with a gradient [79].

A significant advantage of alloyed quantum dots is the ability to fine-tune their optical and electronic properties by adjusting the proportion of materials in the alloy without altering the QD size [80]. This feature is particularly beneficial for applications requiring a specific nanomaterial size. Thus, multicomponent quantum dots are versatile in modifying properties while maintaining a consistent crystallite size.

A common example of alloyed quantum dots is the combination of CdS and CdSe to create CdSxSe1-x/ZnS quantum dots, typically with a diameter of 6 nm [81,82]. By varying the proportions of Selenium (Se) and Sulfur (S) in the core, the wavelength of emitted light can be adjusted, making these quantum dots suitable for specific applications in light-emitting devices and detection systems.

Carbon dots, also known as carbon nanoparticles, C-Dots, or CDs, are nanoparticles primarily composed of carbon atoms with various surface passivation strategies. The scientific community has shown great interest in these nanomaterials due to their remarkable optical absorption and fluorescence emission properties, offering a non-toxic alternative to the metallic quantum dots discussed earlier [83,84]. Despite their differences, carbon quantum dots and conventional semiconductor QDs exhibit a high degree of phenomenological and mechanistic similarity.

When bulk carbon is reduced to the nanoscale, significant electronic properties change, similar to those observed in gold nanoparticles [85]. While other carbon nanomaterials like fullerenes (zero-dimensional), nanotubes (one-dimensional), and graphene (two-dimensional) are well-known, carbon dots possess a unique feature: their properties can be modulated through surface passivation [86]. CDs have a surface rich in defects that can trap electrons and holes following photoinduced charge transfers. Surface passivation helps facilitate radiative recombinations, enabling these nanomaterials to exhibit bright photoluminescence emissions and support other photo-excited state processes, such as redox activities and photodynamic effects [43].

Three main types of carbon dots are distinguished by their structure: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). CQDs are particularly exciting and will be discussed in detail in this manuscript. However, to ensure clarity and avoid ambiguities, it is important to define their characteristics.

GQDs were first synthesized in 2008 by Ponomarenko et al. [87], and they quickly garnered attention due to their unique properties. These nanomaterials have "disk-like" morphologies with a graphene lattice structure, confined within quasi-spherical spaces less than 100 nm in diameter and with layers typically less than 10 nm thick [88].

A notable characteristic of GQDs is their significant quantum confinement and edge effects resulting from the nanocrystal's size, distinguishing them from graphene [89]. While graphene has an infinite exciton Bohr radius, its size limits the electron distribution in GQDs, creating a non-zero bandgap. This quantum confinement allows GQDs to behave as semiconductors or insulators, in contrast to the semimetal nature of bulk graphene [90].

Like CQDs, GQDs can be doped with various heteroatoms to modify their properties. Nitrogen is commonly used to enhance the solubility and optical properties of GQDs, producing a significant edge effect characterized by abundant functional groups on the surface [91,92].

GQDs can be synthesized using two main approaches: top-down and bottom-up methods. In top-down methods, strategies involve cutting graphene-related materials such as graphene oxide [93], carbon nanotubes [94], carbon fibers [95], black carbon [96], graphite powder [97], and coal [98], among others. Bottom-up methods, on the other hand, use smaller graphene-like polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon molecular precursors, such as benzene [99], hexa-peri-hexa-benzocoronene [15], glucose [33], and fullerene [100], among others, which are converted into GQDs through a stepwise chemical reaction. Due to the nature of the precursors, GQDs typically exhibit higher crystallinity than CQDs, which often have more sp2 carbon hybridization and less crystallinity [101]. Consequently, GQDs possess graphene lattices with uniform and ordered crystalline structures resembling single- or few-layer graphene.

The terms “Carbon Nanodots" and "Carbon Quantum Dots" are often used interchangeably, but it is essential to distinguish between these two types of nanomaterials. This document will delve into carbon quantum dots in detail. Carbon nanodots are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with an amorphous core, unlike the crystalline core found in carbon quantum dots. The absence of a crystalline lattice is the key morphological feature that sets CNDs apart [102].

Despite their lack of crystalline structure, carbon nanodots exhibit fluorescent properties, with strong absorption in the blue and ultraviolet ranges and high quantum yields that improve as synthesis techniques advance. They also share several properties with carbon quantum dots, such as high water solubility, resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity, and high biocompatibility [103]. Due to the similarities in their properties, some researchers do not emphasize the difference in core structure and often refer to carbon nanodots and carbon quantum dots collectively as spherical CDs [20]. However, recognizing the distinct amorphous nature of carbon nanodots is crucial for understanding their unique characteristics and potential applications.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are hybrid materials combining polymer and carbon nanomaterials, synthesized using traditional bottom-up methods but with the distinct step of undergoing polymerization before incomplete carbonization [104]. These nanomaterials feature a core-shell structure, with hydrophilic polymeric chains enveloping a hydrophobic core that resembles a three-dimensional mesh or lattice [30]. The synthesis process begins with polymers or biomass containing functional groups, which undergo condensation through dehydration during the initial stages of carbonization [105]. The core's cross-linking degree depends on the reaction conditions, resulting in crosslinked polymeric clusters that fully dehydrate during carbonization, forming a highly hydrophobic core. The temperature and duration of carbonization play crucial roles in defining the core-polymer structure of CPDs. CPDs are characterized by three fundamental aspects: the presence of abundant functional groups and short polymer chains on the exterior due to incomplete carbonization, polydispersity in structure and properties caused by irregularities in polymerization and reaction conditions, and a highly crosslinked core structure formed through dehydration and carbonization [106]. Despite their structural differences from graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs), CPDs exhibit notable fluorescent properties. Numerous functional groups contribute to their light emission, facilitated by the highly crosslinked structure that brings these groups into proximity. This structure allows these groups to act as luminescent centers or form aromatic structures that enhance organic luminescence [105]. Additionally, the structure can restrict the vibration and rotation of luminescent centers, thereby reducing nonradiative transitions and enhancing luminescence.

CQDs were accidently discovered in 2004, by Xu et al. [107] while preparing carbon nanotubes. Since their discovery, extensive research has been conducted across various fields due to their remarkable optical and electronic properties. Typically, CQDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles with both amorphous and crystalline cores [108]. Their crystallinity is lower than that of graphene quantum dots but higher than that of carbon nanodots. These nanoparticles are composed of graphitic or turbostratic carbon with an sp2 configuration and may also consist of graphene or graphene oxide sheets fused or arranged through sp3 hybridized carbon atoms [109]. A key characteristic of CQDs is the abundance of functional groups on their surface, conferring high water solubility and providing active sites for functionalizing the original carbon quantum dot with various materials, enhancing their versatility and applicability in different fields. The following sections will discuss the properties and characteristics of CQDs in detail.

As mentioned earlier, two synthesis routes have been employed for obtaining CQDs: top-down, in which macromolecules or high-molecular-weight compounds are cleaved to obtain the small quantum dots, and bottom-up, in which small precursors are assembled to form the CQDs. Below, each of these strategies is specified. Fig. 2 shows the classification of the different routes for synthesizing CQDs through bottom-up and top-down approaches.

Top-down synthesis procedures depend on aggressive chemical methods, including chemical ablation, laser ablation, electrochemical carbonization, and mechanical milling [110]. These methods divide substantial carbon sources, such as C60 fullerenes, into smaller CQD units, each within the size ranges below 10 nm.

Chemical ablation is a crucial technique for synthesizing CQDs and is known for its effectiveness in producing nano-sized CQDs. This top-down approach involves complex chemical pathways where significant carbon sources, such as C60 fullerenes, undergo controlled disintegration to form smaller, finely-tuned CQD units. The method relies on subjecting the carbon source to harsh chemical environments, ensuring selective breakdown and the creation of CQDs with well-defined characteristics. The choice of chemical agents is crucial in tailoring the synthesis process for specific outcomes to achieve desired size ranges, typically below 10 nm, which are necessary for various applications in nanotechnology [111].

One common strategy in chemical ablation is to employ strong oxidizing agents or acids that react selectively with the carbon precursor. Oxidizing solid acids carbonize small organic molecules into carbonaceous materials, which can be further cut into small sheets by controlled oxidation. A straightforward method for creating luminescent CQDs in an aqueous solution was described by Peng and Travas-Sejdic [112]. This involved dehydrating carbohydrates using concentrated H2SO4, dissolving the carbonaceous materials into individual CQDs using HNO3, and then passivating the mixture with amine-terminated compounds (4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine).

Laser ablation is a precise top-down technique for synthesizing CQDs, using focused laser beams to selectively ablate a carbon target and generate finely tuned CQDs with tailored properties [113]. The method involves intense laser irradiation, inducing localized breakdown of the carbon precursor, leading to high-temperature (>900 °C) and high-pressure (75000 Pa) conditions. This results in the rapid formation of CQDs in a plasma state through vapor crystallization. Laser ablation offers advantages such as exceptional control over particle dimensions, crucial for applications like bioimaging and optoelectronic devices that demand uniformity. The method also enables the production of CQDs with unique optical and electronic properties due to carbon species' controlled and selective excitation [114]. The synthesis of fluorescent CQDs by laser irradiation of a suspension of carbon materials in an organic solvent (HO(CH2CH2O)mH) was reported by Hu et al. [115]. The surface states of the CQDs could be modified to achieve tunable light emission by using organic solvents. The origin of the luminescence was attributed to surface states related to the ligands on the surface of the CQDs based on control experiments (see Fig. 3 a) [115].

Arc discharge is a significant method for synthesizing CQDs, involving the controlled breakdown of carbon-containing materials through gas plasma in a sealed reactor with an anodic electrode. The electric current raises the reactor temperature to 3726 °C, generating high-energy plasma that assembles carbon vapor in the cathode to form CQDs [120]. This top-down approach is praised for producing high-quality CQDs with well-defined crystalline structures and uniform size distributions. Adjusting parameters like carbon precursor, arc current, and gas atmosphere allows tailoring CQD properties [121]. The CQDs produced by this method generally displayed strong water solubility with particle size around 18 nm. This can be attributed to the different scales and low yield of carbon particles generated during the arc-discharge method. By this technique, the as-prepared CQDs can exhibit orange, blue-green, or yellow fluorescence (at 365 nm) [39]. Chao-Mujica et al. [121] used a submerged arc-discharge process to create CQDs in water. The obtained CQDs had a quantum yield of 16 % and emitted two consistent bands in the spectrum of 320–340 nm (band A) and 400–410 nm (band B), respectively, with excitation wavelengths of 275 nm (band A) and 285 nm (band B).

The electrochemical carbonization process unfolds through a meticulously orchestrated electrochemical reaction, where a chosen carbon precursor—often an organic molecule or polymer—is subjected to controlled electrochemical conditions. This involves the application of a potential across an electrode immersed in an electrolyte housing the carbon source [122]. Under these controlled electrochemical settings, the precursor material's carbonization occurs, culminating in CQDs forming. The hallmark advantage of electrochemical carbonization lies in its unparalleled tunability. Researchers wield precise control over the CQD properties by manipulating key parameters such as applied potential, electrolyte composition, and reaction duration [123,124]. This tunability facilitates the customization of CQDs with specific sizes, surface functionalities, and optical properties tailored to distinct application requirements.

Electrochemical carbonization operates under comparatively mild reaction conditions (ambient temperature and pressure), a feature beneficial for preserving functional groups' structural integrity on the surface of CQDs. This attribute broadens the scope of applicable carbon precursors and enhances the compatibility of the method with diverse molecular structures. Under standard electrochemical carbonization conditions, the alcohols were converted into CQDs [125]. To this approach, the reference electrode was a calomel electrode fixed on a freely adjustable Luggin capillary, while the working and auxiliary electrodes were two Pt sheets. As the applied potential increases, so do the CQDs' sizes and degrees of graphitization. The resulting amorphous core CQDs showed excellent size- and excitation-dependent PL properties. Fig. 3b provides an additional example [116]. A three-electrode setup was used in this work to produce CQDs: a working electrode (WE) that was graphite, a counter electrode (CE) that was platinum foil or Pt wire, and a reference electrode (RE) that was Ag/AgCl [116]. The inherently electrochemical nature of the synthesis process aligns with sustainability goals, particularly when utilizing green electrolytes and renewable energy sources. However, better size control could improve the technique.

In the combustion route, the synthesis process commences with a carbon-rich precursor, often an organic compound or biomass-derived material. This precursor undergoes combustion in the presence of an oxidizing agent. The combustion method produced small polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) that are an existential hazard. Citric acid was used to create fluorescent GQDs, which were then functionalized with carboxyl groups via acetic acid moiety conjugation at high temperatures. GQDs with a uniform size of 8.5 nm and abundant carboxyl groups on the surface were created [126]. Furthermore, a single combustion stage was utilized to develop N/S-co-doped CQDs effectively from cellulose-based bio-waste willow catkin. The produced CQDs are uniformly sized and contain many carboxyl groups in their structure [127]. The combustion route is a promising method for CQD synthesis, offering advantages in simplicity, cost-effectiveness, scalability, and versatility in precursor selection. However, for optimal outcomes in CQD applications, challenges related to limited control over size and uniformity, the potential presence of functional groups and impurities, and the effects of high combustion temperatures must be addressed.

Microwave pyrolysis is an efficient method for synthesizing CQDs, using microwave irradiation to induce controlled pyrolytic processes. This innovative technique offers advantages in speed, energy efficiency, and precise engineering of the resulting QDs properties [128]. Heating occurs at the atomic level due to interactions between polar molecules and electric/magnetic fields from microwave irradiation. This method is notable for its rapid reaction, environmental friendliness, energy and time savings, minimal impurity, improved safety features, excellent control over reaction conditions, and good reproducibility compared to other heating techniques [129,130].

Khan et al. [111] described in their state of the art, a simple microwave pyrolysis method to create CQDs. The technique involves mixing poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG200) with a saccharide (such as glucose, fructose, etc.) in water to create a transparent solution, which is then heated in a microwave oven. The obtained CQDs showed PL property that was dependent on excitation. The authors reported 87 % QY of the CQDs. In another work, pulp-free lemon juice and pulp-free onion juice were mixed with deionized water and ammonium hydroxide solution (see Fig. 3c). CDs displayed satisfactory optical proprieties, a high quantum yield of 23.6 %, excellent water solubility, and an average diameter of 6.15 nm.

Hydrothermal and solvothermal methods involve controlled nanocrystal growth in high-temperature, high-pressure aqueous or organic environments. The choice depends on the desired CQD properties and applications. Hydrothermal methods use high-pressure, high-temperature conditions for controlled nucleation and growth in metal cation and ligand precursor solutions. Solvothermal synthesis is similar but uses non-aqueous solvents, impacting CQD size, shape, and surface properties. Solvothermal methods are helpful for water-sensitive precursors or when specific solvents are needed to control reaction kinetics and crystal growth [131]. A standard synthesis method involved dissolving 40 mg of glucose and 1100 mg of K3PO4 in 40 mL of deionized (DI) water [118]. After that, the solution was put in an autoclave chamber lined with Teflon, and for 1 h, N2 gas was blasted into it to extract the dissolved O2. To ensure a hydrothermal reaction occurred, the chamber was sealed and put in an oven set to 200 °C for 12 h (Fig. 3d). After obtaining the supernatant, it was gathered and freeze-dried. The resulting powder was then redispersed in ethanol using a vortex to create a yellow, blue, and green suspension, and the dispersion was left to stand for 2 h to precipitate the free salt. In a different study, m-phenylenediamine (MDPA) and alkali lignin (AL) derived from spruce were combined to create CQDs through hydrothermal carbonization. Under ideal circumstances, these CQDs displayed blue-green fluorescence (FL) with excitation/emission wavelengths of 390/490 nm.

By a hydrothermal method, CQDs were synthesized using cyanobacteria. The potential process is proposed in Fig. 3e. It is reasonable to assume that the cyanobacteria first experienced hydrolysis under thermal conditions and produced large amounts of amino acid, N-acetylglucosamine acid, and N-acetylmuramic acid. Proteins and peptidoglycan reach Cyanobacteria. During the synthetic process, polymerization took place. The soluble polymers produced were then carbonized, which helped carbon cores form and grow. Furthermore, the surfaces of the CQDs were more likely to attach multiple functions because complex compounds participated in the synthetic reaction. With a quantum yield of 9.24 %, it was monodispersed at 2.48 nm and exhibited excitation-dependent emission behavior.

Green synthesis is one of the latest approaches to synthesizing CQDs, which involves using various raw materials, particularly different types of waste. Materials such as human hair [132], orange peels [133], and coal [134] are being used as carbon precursors considered for the synthesis of CQDs synthesis. These organic residues contain significant amounts of amino, sulfur, carboxyl, and hydroxyl functional groups that, depending on the synthesis method, can remain on the surfaces of CQDs. This enhances photoluminescent effects, water solubility, surface passivation and functionalization possibilities [135]. Essentially, traditional synthesis protocols are applied with organic waste as the precursors. Table 1 summarizes commonly used residual organic precursors for obtaining CQDs, categorizing them into vegetable, animal, and industrial origins and correlating the synthesis conditions with the quantum yield of the CQDs.

Based on the data in Table 1, the plots in panels a–d of Fig. 4 were obtained to statistically analyze the recent literature (2020–2024) on CQD synthesis and quantum yield (QY) responses. Among the fifty-two studies included in this analysis, approximately 62 % used plant waste as precursors for CQD synthesis, emphasizing the benefits of green reuse and the biocompatibility of the resulting CQDs (Panel a) [180]. Various plant parts, such as peels [144], fruits [164], and flowers [152] have been employed, with plant residues producing the highest QY values compared to CQDs derived from animal and industrial waste, achieving up to 76.5 %. In practical terms, QY reflects the photoluminescence efficiency of synthesized CQDs, which determines the concentration needed to achieve significant photoluminescent effects. The higher the QY, the less nanomaterial is required [147]. Animal waste accounted for 22.03 % of the studies, while industrial waste represented 15.25 %. Among these, CQDs derived from animal waste exhibited lower QY values, often below 1 %.

In the case of animal sources, materials such as oyster shells, human waste (like hair and skin), animal manure, and milk residues have been reported. Synthesis methods include hydrothermal treatment, microwave processing, and pyrolysis. The highest QY values for animal-derived CQDs were achieved using hydrothermal treatment of cow milk and shrimp shells, with values around 38 % and 27 %, respectively [172]. However, challenges associated with most animal sources include low QY percentages [167], impurities requiring additional purification steps, and size variability of the CQDs [121,177,178]. For industrial waste, the highest QY values were reported from glycerol, with values around 49.9 % achieved via the microwave method [174]. Overall, plant residues tend to produce CQDs with higher luminescent properties, up to twice as much, and are more biocompatible and soluble, which has led to significant scientific interest in these synthesis methodologies.

Panel b illustrates the distribution of synthesis methods, demonstrating that hydrothermal and microwave techniques are the most used due to their simplicity and efficacy. The best yields have been obtained using hydrothermal synthesis with maize starch at 190 °C for 12 h. However, the exact role of the synthesis method in determining QY remains unclear, with an optimal balance of time and temperature appearing more relevant, such as high temperatures for shorter durations.

To further elucidate these effects, panels c and d were constructed. The QY analysis shows similar mean values when comparing synthesis routes Electrochemical, Arc discharge, Pyrolysis and Laser ablation. The main differences are observed when Microwave and Hydrothermal are employed, with QY values reaching up to 76 %. On the other hand, the effect of temperature is inconclusive, possibly due to its combined influence on other operating variables (reaction time, pressure, etc.) and precursor sources (animal, plant, or industrial). However, it is notable that a temperature of 190 °C is associated with the highest reported QY%, highlighting its effectiveness in the synthesis process.

Depending on the degree of sp2 carbon occurrence in the core, CQDs are known to have core-shell structures that are either graphitic crystalline (sp2) or amorphous (mixed sp2/sp3) in the literature. Some researchers reported graphitic crystalline (sp2) cores. The cores are remarkably small, ranging in size from 2 to 3 nm, with a typical lattice spacing of 0.2 nm. The categorization of the cores is based on the synthesis method, the precursors employed, and additional synthetic parameters (like temperature, pH, and duration). Amorphous cores are produced at lower temperatures unless sp2/sp3-hybridized C is present in the precursor, in which case the graphitization (sp2) structure is typically obtained at reaction temperatures above 300 °C [40]. A variety of instrumental methods, including Raman spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM or High Resolution (HR) TEM, and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), are used to ascertain the core structure of CQDs. TEM or SEM measures the CQDs' size and morphology. Whether CQDs are amorphous or crystalline can be determined from the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns. The XRD pattern also determines the crystal structure of CQDs. While two broad peaks occur at 2θ: 25° and 44°, indicating a low-graphitic carbon structure analogous to (002) and (100) diffraction, the broad peak at 23° indicates the amorphous nature of CQD. Fig. 5 a shows the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) and corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) image of the synthesized CQDs by He et al. [181]. The interlayer distances of 0.212 and 0.337 nm matched (100) and (002) planes, indicating that the CQDs exhibited good crystallinity. Fig. 5b shows FTIR and Raman analysis of the same sample. When the Raman spectrum of graphene was compared, the presence of the D-band at 1340 cm−1, the D + G band at 2909 cm−1, and the broadening of the G-band at 1576 cm−1 for CQDs revealed that the oxidation, increase in structural defects, and attachment of functional groups on the C basal plane were the reasons behind the decrease in the size of the in-plane sp2 domains. The I2G/ID intensity ratio and the 2D peak intensity of CQDs, which was approximately 2700 cm−1, were diminished, demonstrating the graphitization of CQDs. The FT-IR spectrum displayed in Fig. 5c provides information about the functional groups in the CQDs. The stretching vibration of –OH was represented by a strong and broad peak at 3332 cm−1, while the flexural vibration of –OH was represented by a peak at 1620 cm−1. Hydroxyl groups were also indicated by the C–OH stretching vibration peak at 1067 cm−1. The peak represented the stretching vibration of C=O in carbonyl and carboxyl groups at 1716 cm−1, while –the peak also represented COOH at 1384 cm−1. Furthermore, the presence of C–O–C and C–N was indicated by the peaks at 1259 and 1245 cm−1, respectively. As a result, the synthesized CQDs had a structure similar to graphene oxide. Considering the robust characterization of CQDs, typical structures can be proposed by molecular dynamics (MD) simulations [40]. Finally, Fig. 5d shows a typical AFM image of a CQD containing a 3.1 nm surface roughness [54].

CQDs display notable optical properties, including distinct absorption bands, photoluminescence, fluorescence, and phosphorescence. Typically, CQDs exhibit two visible absorption bands around 280 and 350 nm, along with a broad tail in the ultraviolet region, as shown in Fig. 5e [183]. The absorption band at 350 nm is attributed to the n-π∗ transition of the C=O bond, while the 280 nm band is due to the π-π∗ transition of the C=C bond. Surface passivation or modification can impact these absorption characteristics [184], which also vary slightly depending on the raw precursor and synthesis technique. Doping CQDs can further alter the absorption wavelength [185].

Surface passivation, heteroatom doping, and functional group doping can all modify the optical properties of CQDs [43]. Surface passivation involves using materials like thiols, thionyl chloride, spiropyrans, and oligomers to create a protective layer on the CQD surface [186]. This layer insulates CQDs from impurity adhesion, providing stability and enhancing fluorescence from the visible to the near-IR range, significantly increasing quantum yields (QYs) by 55–60 % [112]. The absorbance of CQDs can shift to longer wavelengths (350–550 nm) after surface passivation with agents like 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA). The particle size and absorption wavelength are related, with larger CQDs exhibiting longer absorption wavelengths. Functional groups such as amines, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl can be covalently bonded to the CQD surface, improving properties such as photoreversibility, stability, low toxicity, and biocompatibility [11,43].

Doping or co-doping with heteroatoms like B, N, P, S, fluorine (F), and chloride (Cl) is an effective way to alter the absorption spectrum of CQDs. The dopant modifies the π-π∗ energy level (related through the core-sp2 carbon system), which changes the bandgap, electronic structure, and, ultimately, the optical properties of CQDs [187]. The band gap of the CQDs gradually increased from 2.2 to 2.7 eV with increasing N-dopant concentration. In the same way, N doping causes CQDs to become smaller [188]. The novel electronic states that the CQDs created caused a decrease in the bandgap of CQDs (to roughly 48–57 %) [187]. The potential of heteroatoms (N, P, S etc.) to modify surface properties by changing carbon atoms' charge distribution and spin density has recently spurred much research in this area. Compared to traditional functionalization techniques, the invasion of heteroatoms into the perfect hexagonal carbon lattice of GQDs causes more structural and electronic distortion, dramatically changing these particles' electronic and optoelectronic features [11]. Table 2 summarizes some studies in the field.

Photoluminescence (PL)—the emission of light following photon absorption—is a fundamental property of CQDs, manifested primarily as fluorescence or phosphorescence. Fluorescence results from radiative transitions between the excited state and the ground state, typically from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), and occurs on a nanosecond timescale. Key factors influencing CQD fluorescence include the carbon core structure, surface passivation or functionalization, surface defects, quantum confinement effects, and emissive surface traps [209]. The incorporation of heteroatoms stabilizes surface defects, enhancing radiative recombination and leading to brighter fluorescence. Most CQDs exhibit tunable emissions due to multiple emissive sites and variations in particle size, while excitation-independent emission is often associated with highly ordered graphitic domains [210].

In contrast, phosphorescence involves a transition from the lowest triplet excited state (T1) to the singlet ground state (S0), a process forbidden by spin selection rules and thus typically slower and less common [211,212].

Importantly, the fluorescence mechanisms of CQDs are not attributed to a single origin but rather result from a complex interplay of their chemical and morphological characteristics. Various explanations have been proposed in the literature, including quantum confinement, surface states, π-conjugation, and self-trapped excitons.

The small size of CQDs induces quantum confinement, discretizing electronic energy levels and widening the band gap [213]. Upon photoexcitation, electrons transition to higher energy levels and return to the ground state by emitting photons [214]. The size of the CQD modulates this confinement, with smaller dots emitting higher-energy (blue) light and larger dots emitting lower-energy (red) light [13]. Quantum confinement may also enhance quantum yield by reducing non-radiative losses [215].

The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups creates emission centers that enhance fluorescence and can induce red-shifted emissions by introducing localized energy states below the π∗ level [43,216]. For instance, emissions in blue-emitting CQDs have been linked to n → π∗ transitions involving C=O bonds [217]. When oxygenated groups bonded to sp2 carbon, such as C-OH and C-O-C, increase, localized electronic states below the π∗ state are enhanced [218]. These new energy levels within the n → π∗ gap reduce the band gap, resulting in a red-shift of the fluorescence as the surface oxygen content increases. It has been observed that other groups with heteroatoms containing C=O and C=N also introduce new energy levels within the gap, causing additional electronic transitions that also lead to a red-shifted emission when electrons in the defect states associated with these groups return to the HOMO [219].

A conjugation system, characterized by the alternation of single and multiple bonds (double or triple), tends to be fluorescent due to the delocalization of π electrons along the structure [220]. This delocalization reduces the energy difference between the HOMO and the LUMO, allowing the absorption of lower energy photons and subsequent fluorescent emission upon returning to the ground state [221]. CQDs exhibit cores with highly conjugated systems, with sp2 domains that decrease the band gap. Furthermore, the π-electron domain can strongly couple with surface electronic states created by oxidation, influencing the overall electronic structure of the CQDs. An increase in the extent of the π-electron domain leads to a decrease in the band gap for π → π∗ transitions, which, along with the possible presence of surface states with small band gaps, explains the red-shifted emission observed in CDs with a higher degree of conjugation or larger size [222].

The phenomenon of self-trapped excitons occurs when an exciton, generated upon light absorption within the CQD structure, interacts with its molecular environment, inducing a distortion and becoming localized [223]. This trapping often occurs at defect sites, low-energy regions, or specific areas of the nanostructure. Once immobilized, self-trapped excitons have a lower probability of migrating to sites where energy would be dissipated without light emission [224]. This confinement creates a strong local potential field that hinders the movement of charge carriers and suppresses non-radiative relaxation, thus favoring radiative recombination with the emission of a fluorescent photon [225].

A commonly measured parameter for comparing the optical properties of different CQDs is the fluorescence Quantum Yield (QY), This parameter quantifies how efficiently a material converts absorbed light into emitted fluorescence [226]. In other words, it measures the probability that a particle, upon absorbing a photon and becoming excited to a higher energy state, will return to its ground state by emitting another photon rather than losing energy through non-radiative processes such as vibrational relaxation or internal conversion [227]. The QY is defined by:

In practical terms, a material with a QY of 1 (or 100 %) would emit a photon for every photon absorbed, whereas lower QY values indicate that a portion of the absorbed energy is dissipated through non-radiative mechanisms [228]. For the applications discussed in subsequent sections, achieving CQDs with high QY is crucial, as higher QY results in brighter fluorescence signals, facilitating and improving signal detection.

Surface characteristics significantly influence the QY, particularly by modulating non-radiative decay pathways [229]. In nanomaterials such as CQDs, a considerable fraction of atoms resides at the surface, where defects, dangling bonds, and interactions with the surrounding environment can create non-radiative channels for excited electron energy loss [41]. Surface passivation, through coatings with inorganic shells or organic ligands, has been demonstrated as an effective strategy to eliminate surface defects and suppress non-radiative energy loss [230].

Additionally, CQDs exhibit a quantum confinement effect due to their semiconductor nature. When the particle size is smaller than the Bohr exciton radius of the bulk material, the electronic energy levels become quantized into discrete states, similar to those of atoms [231]. Quantum confinement leads to an increase in bandgap energy and enhances the oscillator strength of electronic transitions. Generally, a higher oscillator strength corresponds to a faster radiative decay rate, which, if dominant over non-radiative processes, results in an increase in QY [232].

The most widely employed method for determining QY is the relative method in which the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs is compared to a reference standard with a known QY. For the measurement, the absorbance of a dilute sample, generally with absorbances less than 0.1 to avoid reabsorption, and the standard are measured at the same excitation wavelength [193]. Then, the area under the fluorescence emission curve of both is measured and the QY is calculated as follows:

is the refractive index of the solvent. Initially, a common standard used for CQDs is quinine sulfate in H2SO4, particularly for blue-green emitting CQDs [233]. Other standards, such as rhodamine 6G in ethanol, fluorescein in NaOH, and indocyanine green, have also been employed, depending on the proximity of their emission wavelength to that of the CQDs being measured [234,235].

Phosphorescence in CQDs, first reported by De et al. [236] occurs when CQDs are excited by ultraviolet light, leading to light emission over longer timescales. For instance, CQDs dispersed in a polyvinyl alcohol matrix exhibited phosphorescence with a maximum emission at 500 nm and an average lifetime of 380 ns when excited at 325 nm [237]. Phosphorescence intensity can be influenced by factors such as pH levels, with higher pH reducing phosphorescence due to protonation and the disruption of hydrogen bonds [238]. Novel methods, such as microwave synthesis, have produced ultra-long phosphorescent CQDs (P-CQDs) with yellow-green phosphorescence lasting up to 9 s. These P-CQDs have been used for time-resolved imaging and chemical and biological sensing [182]. Overall, the optical properties of CQDs, including absorbance, photoluminescence, fluorescence, and phosphorescence, can be finely tuned through surface passivation, doping, and functionalization, making them highly versatile for various applications.

Although this document focuses on the optical properties of CQDs, it is important to clarify that other types of CDs also exhibit notable optical properties. The excitation and emission ranges largely depend on the precursors used, synthesis methods, nanoparticle size, and surface functionalization. Table 3 presents the reported ranges for each of the commonly reported types of CDs in the literature.

Stability of CQDs can be affected by several external factors, including UV light, temperature, salts, and pH levels. Therefore, the stability of CQDs is a crucial consideration for their practical applications. One important aspect of stability is photostability, which refers to the ability of CQDs to maintain their properties when exposed to radiation, such as UV or visible light [252]. For instance, Kim et al. [253] synthesized organosilane-functionalized blue luminescent carbon dots. Fig. 6 compares the UV stability of these organosilane-functionalized blue luminescent carbon dots when placed in silica versus when embedded in salt crystals. Part (a) shows that under 264 h of UV exposure, CDs in silica rapidly degraded, while S-CDs exhibited much better stability. NaCl S-CDs were the most stable, degrading 15 times slower and retaining 9 times more PL intensity than CDs in silica after stabilization. Part (b) details the first 8 h, showing similar initial decay in S-CDs before their stability varied, with NaCl S-CDs being the most stable and KBr S-CDs the least stable. The study attributes initial S-CD decay to surface-adsorbed CD degradation and longer-term decay to trapped water/oxygen, causing oxidation. Similarly, Li et al. [254] synthesized nitrogen-doped CQDs through a hydrothermal reaction. These N-CQDs exhibited stable fluorescence properties across a wide range of sodium chloride (NaCl) concentrations (0–1 mol L−1) and pH levels (1.0–13.0). Their fluorescence remained consistent even after prolonged exposure to both xenon and ultraviolet light for up to 1.5 h, demonstrating strong resistance to photobleaching. Additionally, the solid N-CQDs maintained stable fluorescence even after six months of storage.

Wang et al. [255] observed that fluorescence intensity decreased when carbon nanodots (CNDs) were exposed to UV light. This decline was attributed to a photochemical process involving oxygen and the surface conditions of the CNDs. Additionally, it was observed that photobleaching, which is the loss of fluorescence intensity under light exposure, was affected by several factors, including the duration and intensity of UV exposure, the concentration of CNDs, and the presence of solvents and oxygen. The authors also noted that encapsulating CNDs in a polymer matrix enhanced their photostability. In contrast, Jv et al. [256] used a solvent-ultrasonic method to fabricate CDs-modified lutein composites. This modification improved lutein's photostability, 3.4 times under UV light, 3.5 times under visible light, and air retention from 3.6 % to 68 % over 25 days. The presence of CDs enhanced lutein's photoluminescence, indicating electron transfer and FRET. These studies collectively highlight that while some CQDs exhibit excellent photostability under UV irradiation, others may suffer from photobleaching, leading to a loss of fluorescence intensity. This underscores the importance of synthesis methods and environmental factors.

Colloidal stability, the ability of CQDs to remain well-dispersed in a solvent and resist aggregation or precipitation, is another crucial aspect of their behavior [252]. Surface chemistry plays a significant role in this property. Modifying the surface of CQDs with different functional groups can introduce electrostatic or steric repulsion, which helps prevent particle clumping [257]. For instance, extreme pH conditions can alter the surface charge of CQDs, reducing repulsive forces and promoting aggregation [252]. Additionally, high ionic strength in a solution can screen the electrostatic charges on CQDs, diminishing interparticle repulsion and leading to aggregation [254]. Therefore, carefully managing the surface chemistry and solution conditions is essential for maintaining the colloidal stability of CQDs. Baragau et al. [258] synthesized S-functionalized carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) using a hydrothermal method, demonstrating excellent colloidal stability in both aqueous and brine solutions. The high stability of S-CQDs was confirmed by zeta potential analysis, which recorded a value of −42.3 mV, compared to the control sample g-CQD, which had a lower zeta potential of −25.8 mV. As shown in Fig. 7 , UV–Vis spectrophotometry was used to monitor the CQDs in solution over 30 days, revealing good colloidal stability for both S-CQDs and g-CQDs in water. However, while g-CQDs aggregated and deposited in the brine solution, S-CQDs maintained excellent colloidal stability in the brine.

Regarding thermal stability, CQDs exhibit remarkable results, with some able to withstand high temperatures, even exceeding 800 °C [259,260]. Additionally, certain CQDs can retain their luminescence at temperatures up to 90 °C and maintain their mass even at 400 °C [261,262]. CQDs also demonstrate strong pressure stability, preserving their structural and functional integrity in high-pressure environments, such as those encountered in oil and gas exploration [263], as confirmed by studies conducted under reservoir-simulated pressures [264–266].

The following section contains remarkable data published in the last few years regarding CQD applications in sensing, biosensing, bioimaging, photocatalysis, nanomedicine, and petroleum operations (see Fig. 8 ). Each section is accompanied by a systematic analysis of the collected data to highlight the main findings of each sector.

CQDs function as optical chemical sensors that measure the physical and/or chemical properties of an analyte and convert these properties into detectable signals, such as fluorescence emission or light absorption [267]. These sensors are highly sensitive, often capable of detecting a single analyte molecule. Furthermore, the magnitude of the response is typically proportional to the concentration of the analyte, ensuring accurate and reliable measurements [268]. When the analyte interacts with the sensor, a transducer is required to convert the response of that interaction into a measurable signal [269]. In the case of CQDs, various analytical tools are employed for this purpose, such as ultraviolet–visible signal measurements, fluorescence, colorimetric methods, and near-infrared and Raman spectroscopy. These techniques are commonly used as a detection strategy. For a detailed understanding of this application, Fig. 9 presents a schematic summary of the quenching phenomenon between the CQDs and the analyte (with iron (III) as the analyte in this case). The fluorescence emitted by the CQDs is diminished or extinguished in the presence of the analyte.

The most expanded application of CQDs as sensors has focused on detecting metal ions. In the work developed by Singh et al. [271], CQDs are synthesized using a pyrolysis treatment with mango leaves (Mangifera indica) for the detection of Fe2+ in water, obtaining a significant result with a low detection limit of 0.62 ppm. This result is promising since other works like those developed by Nan et al. [272], show detection limits up to 1000 ppm for Fe2+ with CQDs synthesized from plants.

Other analytes have also been detected using CQDs. In 2020, Marilena developed composites from CQDs and NiO by hydrothermal carbonization for methane detection [273]. In this case, the composite was tested at different temperatures and concentrations. It was determined that 150 °C and 1 % were optimal for CH4 detection, with a methane detection limit of 0.1 ppm and a high degree of selectivity when testing the system in the presence of ammonia, CO2 and acetone. Another exciting application of CQDs as sensors is the one reported by Huo et al. [274]. In their work, the authors developed a pH sensor using Poly(m-phenylene isophthalamide) as a substrate and CQDs as probes for detection. The authors quantified color change and fluorescence intensity modification as parameters for pH determination in aqueous solutions over a wide pH range, with response times of less than 5 s.

Some of the most recent studies involving CQDs have made significant efforts to include, in addition to traditional carbon and nitrogen precursors, other elements that enhance the optical behavior of the CQDs, such as rare earth elements like europium, as employed by Gan et al. [275]. Including these elements is based on the remarkable fluorescent behavior that results from the formation of complexes between organic ligands and lanthanide ions (see Fig. 10 a). One of the most interesting outcomes of incorporating these elements in the sensor synthesis process is the generation of a second emission peak in the fluorescence spectrum of the CQDs. This second peak generally enhances the sensitivity of the measurement when detecting an analyte and reduces interference issues, as both signals can be used for tracking. In this case, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-Eu increases with the presence of mercury. As the authors mention, the presence of the two emission peaks allows for monitoring across a wider concentration range since the first peak, associated with the CQDs, increases more at higher concentrations, while the second emission peak, associated with europium, is more prominent at lower concentrations. The authors utilize this optical behavior to construct a linear calibration curve, correlating mercury concentration with the sum of intensity changes at the two emission wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 10b, the relationship is linear with correlation coefficients of 0.9934 in a mercury concentration range of 1 nM–20 nM and a quantification limit of 0.2 nM. These concentrations are significantly lower than other studies, where the reported quantification limits range from 4 nM [276] to 220 nM [277]. Achieving such low detection limits meets the requirements for quantifying this metal in food and environmental samples. According to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, the maximum allowable mercury concentration in drinking water is 10 nM [278], improving these CQDs efficiently.

The performance of CQDs alone for sensing certain analytes has opened the door to developing sophisticated devices where fluorescence intensity modifications are not the only strategy used for tracking. One of the most innovative applications was developed by Xuemei et al. [279], in which nanocomposites of CQDs with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) were synthesized. The produced nanocomposite was used to modify a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) to detect iron in oxidation states II and III using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), as is represented in Fig. 10c. The results showed that once the parameters of amplitude (0.15V) and potential increment (0.004V) were optimized, the detection of iron II and III was carried out in simulated gastric fluid (SGF). The differential pulse voltammograms (Fig. 10d) show a reduction peak at 0.22V attributed to iron III, which is separated from the iron II peak at 0.6V. Additionally, the possibility of simultaneously detecting iron II and III was evaluated. The graph shows that the qualitative identification of both ionic species is possible. In this case, the peaks are reduced, likely due to competition between ions for the binding sites on the electrode surface. The development of such devices demonstrates a technological advancement where CQDs are employed to enhance iron detection systems. Developing equipment for more selective and lower concentrations is an important research subject today.

Sensors with biotechnological applications have also been developed using CQDs. Han et al. [280]. Developed xylan-based CQDs to produce highly selective and reductive detectors capable of electrochemically detecting dopamine. Dopamine is a molecule of clinical interest for the diagnosis of diseases such as Parkinson's and other neurodegenerative diseases. Under optimal operating conditions, the linear detection range was between 0.1 and 300 μM, with a detection limit of 1.59 nM. Another important approach was reported by Anjila et al. [281], who developed a method for detecting melamine, a common adulterant in milk and dairy products. They employed terbium-doped GQDs to achieve sensitive and selective detection at concentrations as low as 5 μM. The detections were performed in real-time using a rapid and sensitive spectroscopic approach. Table 4 summarizes recent work on using CQDs for the optical detection of different molecules or drugs.

The data presented in Table 4 were compiled from recent literature published between 2020 and the present. A total of forty-one articles were gathered for statistical analysis. According to the studies reviewed, heavy metals are the most frequently detected analytes using CQDs, accounting for 42 % of the total (see Panel a, Fig. 11 ). This prevalence is likely due to the electron transfer mechanisms between CQDs and various metal ions, which allow for easy detection of the signal decrease in fluorescence intensity caused by fluorescence quenching [282]. Among heavy metals, mercury is the most frequently detected and has achieved the lowest quantification limits (0.0002 μM) [275]. In practical terms, the quantification limit indicates the sensitivity achieved in detecting the analyte, as well as the minimum concentration of the analyte in an unknown sample that can be reliably detected. Lower quantification limits demonstrate a high affinity between the CQD and the target analyte. The World Health Organization (WHO) has established a maximum mercury concentration of 0.005 μM in water [283]. Therefore, detecting concentrations below this threshold is both practical and useful for water quality control. For instance, Gan et al. [275] successfully detected mercury at a concentration of 0.0002 μM using CQDs, indicating that these monitoring concentrations are significant for meeting quality standards. For other metals detected, the concentrations that can be monitored are higher: chromium at 0.192 μM [284], copper at 1.57 μM [285], and lead at 0.003 μM [286]. Another group of analytes detected using CQDs has focused on the food sector, where molecules like glucose [287] and lactose [288] are important to track, particularly in cases of cross-contamination for sensitive individuals, such as those with diabetes or lactose intolerance.

Panels b and c in Fig. 11 show the most common synthesis methods and carbon sources used for the CQDs employed as sensors. The most widely used are the hydrothermal method (51 %) and microwave method (24 %), with organic compounds being the most common carbon sources (46 %). As mentioned in Section 3.4, organic compounds are increasingly utilized due to the added value of using waste materials rich in carbon atoms to synthesize quantum dots and the valorization of these byproducts through their implementation. In addition to the remarkable QYs achieved, it is important to note that higher QYs are directly related to more sensitive detection limits. A higher initial fluorescence would allow tracking down to lower concentrations through fluorescence quenching. Considering all the above, it is evident that there has been a strong trend in recent years toward using organic compounds as an efficient source for the synthesis of quantum dots for subsequent applications. Given the heterogeneity of conditions and carbon sources used by different authors, it is valuable to conduct a deeper analysis of the data to identify the variables that significantly influence the performance of CQDs as sensors. Therefore, the correlation between synthesis temperature and method with the quantification limit is presented in Panels d and e, respectively.

Regarding temperature, between 150 °C and 200 °C yields more sensitive CQDs. Below this range, incomplete carbonization reactions may occur, resulting in incomplete or defective crystalline structures that could impair CQD performance [289]. Conversely, higher temperatures could lead to thermal degradation or the formation of nanoparticles with irregular and less uniform sizes [290]. Thus, the 150 °C–200 °C range is optimal for CQD-based sensors. As for the synthesis method, the data indicates that the hydrothermal carbonization method produces CQDs with lower quantification limits. This is likely due to the controlled pressure and temperature conditions that provide more excellent stability during the synthesis process, as well as potentially more efficient functionalization for methods involving surface passivation [291].

One of the significant fields of application of photocatalysis is environmental remediation and water splitting using sunlight. To take advantage of this energy source, it is essential that the photocatalyst can utilize the visible and/or near-infrared spectrum provided by sunlight [322]. Current photocatalytic systems do not employ all these wavelengths because the catalysts absorb energy in more restricted regions [323]. For this reason, CQDs have generated much interest in this sector. In addition to advantages such as low cost, biocompatibility and high-water solubility, CQDs exhibit photoluminescence over a broad spectrum (downward and upward) and the ability to absorb light at long wavelengths and exchange energy through quenching mechanisms with other species in solution-making them excellent candidates for implementation in photocatalytic systems [324]. Photoelectron transfer appears to be the main mechanism by which CQDs improve the performance of a catalyst for more efficient degradation of a pollutant (see Fig. 12 ).

In addition to advantages such as low cost, biocompatibility and high-water solubility, CQDs exhibit photoluminescence over a broad spectrum (downward and upward) and the ability to absorb light at long wavelengths and exchange energy through quenching mechanisms with other species in solution-making them excellent candidates for implementation in photocatalytic systems.

Early applications in this direction focused on improving the efficiency of titanium oxide by using CQDs. Zhao et al. [326] designed CQDs/TiO2 hybrid materials by a coprecipitation method and used them for Cr(VI) degradation. It was found that doping with CQDs accelerates photoelectron transfer and prevents carrier recombination, improving the catalysis efficiency in two aspects. The removal of chromium (VI) and other organics is facilitated by a surface adsorption process of the contaminants on the surface of the CQD/TiO2 hybrid material. When irradiated with visible light, photoelectrons and holes are produced, promoting the reduction of chromium (VI) and the oxidation of organic compounds. Once made, the chromium (III) and other products are desorbed. The authors indicate that factors such as the photocatalytic material's concentration and the medium's pH are crucial to the performance of the photocatalyst. If the concentration is too low, the amount of light absorbed is insufficient. If it is too high, the turbidity of the aqueous solution increases, generating a light scattering phenomenon.

The synergistic effect of CQDs has not only been tested with TiO2 but also with other materials such as carbon nitride. In work by Jourshabani et al. [327], CQDs were attached to nitride nanosheets for the photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B, a widely used dye. The results show that using CQDs increased the reaction rate almost 40 % compared to the base material. In this case, the mechanism involving the CQDs is to keep the electrons and holes generated when light is absorbed separately. If these are not recombined, the effective separation of these charge carriers improves the efficiency of the photocatalytic process. The mechanism by which contaminants present in water are degraded more efficiently in the presence of CQDs is explained by Yang al. [328]. The authors mention that when a semiconductor material is irradiated by visible light, electrons are excited and move from the valence band to the conduction band, leaving holes in the valence band. Usually, these electrons and holes recombine rapidly, and only a portion of the charges can participate in the photocatalytic process.

Two fundamental strategies have been developed to improve the electron transfer performance of CQDs within their structure and thus enhance their photocatalytic activity. One of these strategies consists of doping with heteroatoms within the carbonaceous structure. Houjuan et al. [329] developed nitrogen-doped quantum dots were developed to evaluate the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline. The nanocomposites demonstrated greater photocatalytic degradation capacity. The presence of nitrogen groups allowed for more efficient formation of superoxide and hydroxyl radicals on the surface of the sample, which facilitated the degradation of tetracycline.

A second strategy involves modulation of the surface of the CQDs to improve the rate and selectivity of redox reactions on the photocatalyst surface. Khalid et al.
