[330] used five different functional ligands for surface modification of quantum dots and evaluated the effect of each of these in terms of photoluminescence and charge transfer efficiency. It was observed that the use of mercaptoacetic acid improves the charge transfer efficiency by almost 80 %, and in general, they observed that if the ligands contain thiol groups, the charge transfer is facilitated compared to ligands containing hydroxyl and amine groups. Therefore, surface ligands have a significant influence on the efficiency of CQDs in photocatalytic processes.

An interesting work was reported by Qu et al. [331] where a hybrid ZnO/N, S-CQDs nanoflowers were synthesized through a hydrothermal technique ciprofloxacin degradation, as shown Fig. 13 a. Using L-cysteine as the carbon source and ethylene glycol as a passivation agent, the N, S-CQDs were incorporated into ZnO nanoflowers with highly reactive surfaces. Fig. 13b outlines the synthesis procedure and the proposed degradation mechanisms. In pathway I, the hydroxyl radicals (•OH) substituted a specific site in the ciprofloxacin (CIP) piperazine ring, forming a compound with m/z = 348. The piperazine ring was then opened, undergoing decarboxylation, which led to an intermediate with a five-membered ring (m/z = 274). Further advanced oxidation resulted in smaller intermediates (m/z = 185 and 137). In pathway II, the –F group in CIP was replaced by –OH through superoxide radical (•O2−) and hydroxyl radical attacks, generating a defluorinated product (m/z = 330). This was followed by piperazine ring decyclization and dealkylation, producing intermediates with m/z values of 304 and 302 and deeper oxidation products (m/z = 261, 244, 228). Finally, smaller molecules such as CO2 and H2O were produced. As shown in Fig. 13c, typically, ZnO grows fastest along the [0001] crystal direction. However, HMTA, known for its coordination properties, acted as a capping agent on the (0001) plane of ZnO nanorods during growth. This led to the formation of ZnO nanosheets, primarily exposing (0110) facets. Eventually, these nanosheets formed a nanoflower structure to minimize the system's Gibbs free energy (ΔG). Finally, Fig. 13d shows that the ZnO/N, and S-CQDs achieved ciprofloxacin degradation rates of 92.9 % in simulated light after 20 min and 85.8 % in natural light after 50 min.

Table 5 compiles the most recent papers (2020–2024) in which CQDs have been used for pollutant removal by photocatalytic mechanisms. A total of 45 papers were collected and analyzed. As can be seen in Fig. 14 a, the pollutants that have been photodegraded using CQDs have different chemical natures. About half of the studies (46.7 %) involve the degradation of synthetic probes or dyes, mainly employed by the textile industry. The other pollutants are grouped into organic solvents, organic molecules and drugs, which negatively affect water quality due to their toxicity to aquatic organisms and human health [332,333]. Some solvents, such as benzene and drugs, are toxic even at low concentrations [334]. Another problem associated with these contaminants is their bioaccumulation in the tissues of organisms that encounter them. This can lead to toxic levels in the food chain, affecting wildlife and humans who consume these organisms [335]. When persisting in water, drugs, especially antibiotics such as tetracycline, can promote microbial resistance, generating bacterial strains that are difficult to eradicate [336]. The persistence of these contaminants in the environment is a public health problem, so in recent years, significant efforts have been made to remove them effectively.

For photocatalytic treatments to be economically viable, the use of sunlight as a catalyst for the degradation reactions would be ideal [337]. However, despite significant efforts to use this light source, only 11.4 % of recent studies have succeeded (see Fig. 14b). Nevertheless, visible light is increasingly used (38.6 %), a more economical and viable source than UV light, which is traditionally used in these processes. The optical properties of CQDs have gained momentum in this field, as they can absorb a wide range of wavelengths, including ultraviolet (UV) and visible light [338]. Thus, they can act as effective photosensitizers, initiating photocatalytic reactions under natural illumination.

To better understand the performance of this type of nanomaterials as performance enhancers of traditionally used photocatalysts, Fig. 14c presents the removal percentages achieved with CQDs synthesized using different routes. No statistically significant differences were found in the removal percentages achieved, suggesting that the performance of the CQDs is acceptable (above 90 %) regardless of the synthesis method employed, with the highest values found for the hydrothermal and solvothermal methods (95 %) [354,356]. The results with the highest removal percentages are related to the removal of solvents (xylene [365] and benzene [354]) and drugs (paracetamol [356]) with the use of visible light.

In contrast, the contaminants that degrade with lower removal rates are those classified as probes, such as methyl orange (85 %) [342], congo red (87 %) [360] and orange II (87 %) [355]. These probes comprise conjugated aromatic rings and functional groups such as azogroups (-N=N-). The high degree of conjugation gives them greater stability against degradation due to the high activation energies required to break these types of double bonds [383]. In addition, these dyes can absorb visible light effectively, which gives them their usefulness as indicators and probes in various applications. However, this characteristic also gives them higher resistance to photodegradation since they can dissipate the absorbed energy non-reactively, such as heat [384].

It should be remembered that in photocatalytic processes, degradation involves the generation of free radicals, such as hydroxyl radicals, which can attack the pollutant [385]. The high electronic resonance of these probes makes them less susceptible to attack compared to simpler molecules such as solvents or drugs. Finally, these dyes may interfere with the photocatalytic process by absorbing light and decreasing the proportion of light that activates the photocatalyst, thus reducing the formation of reactive species necessary for degradation [386].

Regarding the type of photocatalyst employed in the enhanced photodegradation studies (Fig. 14d), similarities are observed in the removal values achieved. This indicates that the CQDs would be strongly involved in improving the adsorption process and not interacting with any photocatalysts. The overall analysis of the data suggests that the photodegradation of contaminants is enhanced in all cases when CQDs are used in the process and that some contaminants, such as drugs and solvents, are degraded more efficiently.

The remarkable optical performance of QDs captured the attention of numerous researchers for their use in bioapplications as a replacement for the organic fluorescent probes traditionally employed. However, the high toxicity of QDs has implied notorious limitations in the intended bioapplications of this type of fluorescent nanomaterial. One way to overcome these limitations is by using CQDs as an alternative. Properties such as their high solubility in water, excitation and emission lengths in the visible range, high QYs and photostability make them promising candidates for various applications involving in vivo assays. Different types of CQDs have been implemented with promising results for bioimaging; at the cellular level, they allow obtaining a luminous contrast background in which cells are distinguished, or, on the contrary, they manage to penetrate cellular structures for detailed observation (see Fig. 15 a).

In the study by Xu et al. [387] used diphenyl semi carbazide-CQDs, synthetized by hydrothermal route for the morphological observation of DU 145 brain cells. The cells were incubated in a fresh culture medium containing 100 μL of a CQD suspension (1 mg L−1) for 2 h (Fig. 15b). Confocal microscopy revealed that the incubated cells showed green emissions when irradiated with ultraviolet light. The images showed good photostability and resistance to photobleaching, as they were consistent over 1 h of constant excitation. The same approach has been used in other studies, including one by Zhao et al. [388]. They used the hydrothermal method and water hyacinth to obtain CQDs. The CQDs were stable over the range of salt concentrations tested (0.02–0.2 M NaCl), and exposure to UV light for 60 min did not generate appreciable changes in intensity. Klebsiella cells were incubated for 12 h in a medium containing 500 μg mL−1 of the synthesized CQD (Fig. 15c).

Huang et al. [389] report the development of hybrid materials with CQDs and oxidized cellulose nanocrystals-TEMPO. Cell viability tests were performed using HeLa cells, RAW 264.7 macrophages, and CellTiter-GloTM reagent to quantify ATP as a metabolic indicator (see Fig. 15d). Cell viability was observed to be preserved in a concentration range of 1–1000 μL mg−1 for both cell lines. However, concentrations higher than these significantly affected cell viability, mainly in RAW 264.7, where viability was reduced to 40 % after 24 h of incubation. The cells were incubated for 4 or 24 h with hybrid material for confocal observation of the cells. The interactions between the hybrid material and the cells (HeLa and RAW 264.7 macrophages) were much better than those of the individual materials (CQD or nanocrystals). Observation of the images showed much more intense fluorescence, suggesting a strong cellular association and internalization of the hybrid material.

Kan et al. [111] reported the use of hydrothermal treatment to use CQDs from wheat straw and bamboo hydrolysates. Fibroblast cell viability was initially evaluated by MTT assay with concentrations between 0 and 400 μg mL−1 of CQDs. The results show more than 90 % cell viability when the concentration increases from 25 to 400 μg mL−1, indicating the possibility of use in in vivo imaging due to high biocompatibility. Fig. 15e shows the distribution of CQDs in the mouse body at different times. The images show how the CQDs travel through the body until they reach the tumor location. The dissected organs were inspected with UV light, and a fluorescent signal was detected in those containing tumor cells but not in healthy organs. These results suggest the high potential of CQDs for bioimaging and phototherapy.

In another work, Sobhanan et al. [390] developed CQDs obtained with phenylenediamine and L-cysteine, which can color more specialized structures, such as the nucleolus. The authors observed that after 5 min of incubation, the CQDs could selectively stain nucleoli. No signs of lysosomal/endosomal trapping were evident, and after 24 h of incubation, the staining was still localized to the nucleoli, indicating rapid and selective staining after cell internalization. Fig. 15f shows the nuclei of unfixed (live) cells selectively stained with the CQDs.

Despite the widespread use of CQDs for bioimaging, the precise mechanism by which they manage to internalize into the cell and, in some cases, localize to specific organelles is still unclear. Tiwari et al. [391] used hyaluronic acid-functionalized CQDs to elucidate the mechanism of tumor cell staining using CQDs (see Fig. 15g). They used hyaluronic acid because it is an essential component of the extracellular matrix that could bind to CD44 receptors, which are overexpressed in cancer cells. Two cell lines were used, one with high CD44 expression (HeLa) and one with low expression (MCF-7). Flow cytometry determined that HeLa cells endocytosed many functionalized CQDs, whereas in MCF-7, the uptake was deficient. Thus, CQDs appear to be efficiently uptake by hyaluronic acid receptor-mediated endocytosis and serve as a specific targeting probe, especially in CD44-overexpressing tumor cells. Kumar et al. [246] employed CQDs derived from biomass waste for bioimaging applications. Through confocal microscopy, they demonstrated that the CQDs not only enabled clear cellular visualization but also allowed for the detection of inflammatory processes. Notably, the CQDs exhibited anti-inflammatory properties by downregulating the gene expression of inflammatory markers such as interleukin-6 (IL-6). These findings highlight the potential of biomass-derived CQDs not only as imaging agents but also as candidates for targeted therapies in immune-related diseases. A summary of recent work and its scope for bioimaging using CQDs is presented in Table 6 .

Table 6 compiles a selection of studies conducted between 2020 and 2024 that have considered using CQDs for imaging in different cell lines. One of the main focuses of these investigations is on human cancer cells, these being the most used [431,435,436]. More than half of the papers (57.4 %) have focused on this cell type (see Fig. 16 a). Cancer cells often exhibit distinctive morphological, molecular and functional characteristics compared to normal cells. These differences can be exploited by CQDs, either anchored to biomarkers or used alone, to facilitate their visualization [438]. Part of the interest also lies in developing targeted therapies, such as antibody or gene therapies, allowing observation of how certain drugs interact when used with a fluorescent marker, such as CQDs [439].

Due to the interest in potentially treating cancer cells, recent approaches have focused more on human cells. A significant percentage of studies (34 %) have been performed on healthy human cells. In contrast, simpler models, such as bacterial and animal cells, account for a smaller proportion of related work, 6.4 % and 2.1 %, respectively. CQDs have been widely used as efficient fluorescent markers in the cell lines studied; in more than 60 % of the studies, they were employed (see Fig. 16b). As mentioned throughout this review paper, CQDs exhibit remarkable fluorescent ability. Moreover, they can be excited by a wide range of wavelengths, and their emission at different wavelengths facilitates multiplexing, which allows the use of several colors simultaneously for morphological observation of different organelles [389]. Unlike other heavy metal-based analogs, CQD's low toxicity and biocompatibility give their use enormous potential. Their safety has even led to the consideration of in vivo applications without negative health implications [440].

In terms of emission lengths, the number of CQDs with emissions in the blue and green spectrum is remarkable, being the most common in the literature (34 % and 28.9 %). The emission length often determines the staining capability and the specific organelle to be labeled. Depending on the specific application, different emission lengths allow specialized visualization of organelles or cellular mechanisms, providing adequate visualization and tracking.

In this context, the chemical stability of CQDs and their resistance to photodegradation are key features. In simple terms, this means that they maintain their fluorescence for extended periods under illumination conditions, making it possible to obtain long-term bioimages without signal loss [441].

Fig. 16c shows the color classification according to the emission length of the CQDs used in bio applications. Most of the trials in which bioimaging was obtained used exclusively emissions in the blue and green spectrum (19 % and 6.7 %, respectively). This could be attributed to better QYs and, thus, better resolution images [442]. Photodynamic therapies, on the other hand, were primarily performed with CQDs presenting emissions in the red and infrared spectrum. The emission lengths of these CQDs are longer than those of blue or green CQDs, allowing them to penetrate deeper into biological tissues [443]. In photodynamic therapies, where light must reach cells or deep tissues to activate the photosensitizing agent and produce reactive species that destroy tumor cells, this feature is especially valuable. In addition, light in the red spectrum has lower absorption by natural components of the body, such as water, hemoglobin and melanin, compared to shorter wavelengths [444]. This means that red light propagates more efficiently through tissues. Finally, as noted in this review, the main precursors for synthesizing CQDs are often organic wastes, which implies relatively low costs, representing an essential advantage over other high-cost fluorescent probes.

In the previous section, we discussed applications in which CQDs are used for imaging but have also been used to label specific biomolecules [445]. One critical application of CQDs in biosensing is fluorescence-based detection [446]. As shown in Fig. 17 a, CQDs can be functionalized with biomolecules such as antibodies, DNA, or enzymes to create target specificity [447], among other benefits like antibacterial properties [448,449]. When the target biomolecule is introduced, a binding event occurs, leading to a detectable change in fluorescence, current, or potential, which indicates a successful target detection. Praseetha et al. [450] synthesized CQDs from beetroot extract through the microwave method and characterized them as capable of binding with proteins. This study combined CQDs with Aloe Vera gel via encapsulation to investigate the wound healing mechanisms and the quenching property of the CQDs, in this case, the fluorescence decreased after the conjugation, an effect that indicates the presence of CQDs in the sample and their successful bonding with the aloe. The previously mentioned quenching property can also help detect pesticides; this sensing method often offers a low limit of detection (LOD) at or below the legislative limits. In this context, Li et al. [451] synthesized three CQDs, N-CD-Fla, NS-CD-Fla, and NSB-CD-Fla using nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), boron (B) and flavonoid (Fla) as additives based on the hydrothermal strategy for fenitrothion (Fen), dithianon (Dit) and dinoseb (Din) sensing detection respectively. The three pesticide detectors showed desired stability at pH 7.0 with an incubation time of 2 min to notice changes in the fluorescence intensity, and a limit of detection was estimated to be 0.36 nM for Fen, 0.28 nM for Dit, and 0.66 nM for Din. Fig. 17b–d presents the fluorescence spectra of these three CQD at different pesticide concentrations, showing the intensity decreases with the increase of the pesticide concentration. The quenching efficiency versus the concentration of pesticide is also shown in each figure, displaying a desirable linear relationship from 1.0 nM to 2000 nM in Fig. 17b, from 1.0 nM to 1000 nM in Fig. 17c, and from 1.0 nM to 3000 nM in Fig. 17d. Similar to this study, Yang et al. [452] synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) by hydrothermal method using gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) as an additive for carbendazim detection. The N-CQDs/AuNCs showed stability at pH 6.0 with an incubation time of 8 min and a detection limit of 37.25 μM. In this case, the fluorescent signal around 450 nm belongs to the N-CQDs, which decreases due to the presence of AuNCs and is restored after adding the carbendazim, as shown in Fig. 17e. The measurement is rooted in detecting the Stimulated Raman Scattering (SRS) signal emitted by N-CQDs/AuNCs at approximately 724 nm, a signal that also increases upon introducing carbendazim. This suggests that the particle size of the sensing probe is larger than its initial size.

CQDs are also utilized in Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) biosensors [453] CQDs act as energy donors in these sensors, only transferring energy to an acceptor molecule when nearby. This proximity-based mechanism is used to study molecular interactions, such as protein-protein or protein-DNA interactions, providing insights into cellular processes and disease mechanisms [454,455]. Chawre et al. [456] synthesized nitrogen-doped CQDs with multicolor properties by the hydrothermal method to interact with Gold Nanorods (AuNRs) and detect biomolecules like H2O2, acetylcholinesterase, glutathione, and glucose. AuNRs can quench (turn off) the fluorescence of CQDs through FRET, and it is restored (turn on) when AuNRs are etched. In this case, the sensing system was verified in human plasma samples to detect the H2O2 production, where the redox reaction of Au with H2O2 results in the decomposition of AuNRs, turning on the fluorescence of the CQDs. Based on the description, the scheme defined in Fig. 18 was proposed.

Additionally, CQDs are employed in electrochemical biosensors [457]. Their high surface area and excellent electron transfer capabilities enhance the sensitivity of these sensors. CQD-modified electrodes can detect biological analytes through changes in current or potential, enabling applications in glucose monitoring [458], pathogen detection [459], and environmental monitoring [460]. Overall, the unique properties of CQDs allow a wide range of biosensing applications, from early disease detection [461] and monitoring real-time imaging and portable diagnostics [462]. As research continues, developing new CQD-based biosensors promises to enhance our ability to detect and understand biological phenomena with unprecedented precision and sensitivity. Table 7 summarizes some of the most representative studies conducted between 2020 and 2024 using CQDs to detect various biomolecules.

According to Table 7, the plots in Fig. 19 were designed to systematically analyze the recent literature (2020–2024) on CQD sensing applications and detection limit (LOD) responses. Fifty-one studies were analyzed, of which about 84 % utilized plant-based materials and organic acids in equal measure (42 %) as the main components for synthesizing CQDs. Fig. 19a highlights the preference for natural sources such as citric acid [463] and biomasses like coffee waste [464], milk [465], and carrot juice [466] in CQD synthesis. The choice of these materials may be linked to their sustainability and potential applications in biological and environmental studies [467].

The use of industrial and chemical derivatives accounts for 12 %, including by-products or waste from other industrial processes. For example, diammonium hydrogen citrate [468] can be a by-product from various industrial processes, including the pharmaceutical industry or fertilizer manufacturing [469]. Trisodium citrate dihydrate [470] can also come from the pharmaceutical industry and food additives production [471]. This indicates a revalorization and reuse of materials, making the synthesis more environmentally friendly. Biomaterials or animal waste used to synthesize the CQDs represent 4 %, showing that these sources are less common [180], harder to process if proper management is not carried out [472], or less efficient compared to other sources in terms of an economic or environmental benefit, for example, Crab shell waste was used as a non-toxic resource due to its availability [388].

On the other hand, Fig. 19b shows the particular approach of researchers to detect medicines 27.5 % and pesticides 21.6 % using CQDs; this might be due to its biocompatibility properties and the growing concern about monitoring the pharmaceutics and toxic substances in humans [473], food [474], and the environment [463]. The third group of targets is biomolecules (17.6 %), with Dopamine being one of the most frequently occurring targets in this category [280,464,475,476]. This neurotransmitter is abundant in the brain and regulates a large number of psychological functions in the central nervous system [477], so its detection is of great interest.

The fourth and fifth groups are Bacteria/Viruses and Ions/Chemical Substances, with 9.8 % each, supporting the idea that the focus of researchers is the targets related to human well-being. The results shown in Fig. 19c and d were derived from a systematic analysis using the information presented in Table 7. In this instance, the detection limit serves as the response variable and is characterized based on two parameters: the carbon source and the target biomolecule. These analyses enable the assessment of the interaction between these two parameters and their collective impact on the response variable. Fig. 19c results support the sensing application of CQDs for medicines and pesticides as they demonstrate very low detection limits, all under 1500 μM, making CQDs suitable for detecting these targets. Overall, CQDs have low detection limits for most targets, highlighting their biocompatibility and non-toxic properties. The highest detection limit of approximately 25000 μM is observed in detecting ions/chemical substances, indicating low precision for this target type. However, as shown in Fig. 19b, at 9.8 %, it is the fourth most studied target alongside bacteria/viruses, with a low detection limit.

Based on Fig. 19d, the detection limit for CQDs synthesized from plant materials/biomasses extends up to approximately 25000 μM, indicating a significant challenge in achieving accurate detection due to the high variability in CQD sizes and other physical properties. However, Fig. 19a shows that plant materials are one of the most utilized carbon sources for synthesizing CQDs. Conversely, CQDs obtained from organic acids and other carbon precursors offer more consistent sizes and properties, resulting in smaller detection limits (all below 5000 μM) and, thus, greater precision.

CQDs have garnered significant attention in nanomedicine and drug delivery due to their distinctive optical and electronic properties [505]. Their surface can be easily modified with various ligands, such as antibodies, peptides, or small molecules, to recognize and bind to specific cellular receptors [506,507]. Additionally, they can be engineered to release drugs in response to particular stimuli, such as pH, temperature, or light. This targeting ability ensures that the therapeutic agents are delivered directly to the diseased cells, minimizing side effects and enhancing treatment efficacy. Different mechanisms and ligands have been used in this area. Fig. 20 presents a graphic summary of the use of CQDs in cancer cell therapy.

Zavareh et al. [509] synthesized 5-fluorouracil-chitosan-carbon quantum dot-aptamer (5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt) for specific targeted delivery following pH changes of 5-FU anti-cancer drug used in breast cancer treatment. The average nanoparticle size was 122.7 nm, which could help achieve high drug loading and entrap efficiency. The results showed that 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt was biocompatible and could kill tumor cells efficiently, turning them into a potential nanocarrier in breast cancer treatment.

In addition to imaging and drug delivery, CQDs have applications in theragnostic, which combine therapeutic and diagnostic functionalities in a single platform [510]. Some of the most recent advances where CQDs have been applied for cancer therapy have led the technology to in vivo studies, demonstrating its effectiveness. One such study was conducted by Han et al. [511]. In their work, the researchers reported the development of a novel Doxorubicin (DOX) delivery system, a chemotherapeutic drug widely used for the treatment of different types of cancer, though its effectiveness has been questioned due to its low selectivity for tumor cells [512]. In this regard, DOX's performance was improved by using CQDs synthesized from L-arginine and co-doped with silver and copper (Arg-Ag@Cu). Each element present in the co-doped CQDs had a specific effect: the CQDs contributed to the release of reactive nitrogen species to cause cellular damage, Ag acted as an enhancer of the CQDs' photoluminescence intensity, and the functionalization with Cu2+ served as an anchor for interparticle self-assembly. Once localized at specific sites, the CQDs and Cu generated a release system for reactive nitrogen and oxygen species through the induction of chemo and photodynamic therapy. The authors demonstrated that copper depletion caused a destabilization of the Arg-Ag@Cu complex, allowing the release of DOX to complement the tumor treatment in target cells. The performance of these materials was tested in vivo using 4T1 tumor cells in healthy mice, which were treated with the CQDs complexed with silver and copper. As shown in Fig. 21 a, treatment with Arg-Ag@Cu significantly reduced the area of tumor necrosis in the lungs (∼7 %) compared to the group treated with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (∼55 %) or the control group. An approach to further improve the performance of CQDs in eliminating cancer cells has been the use of photodynamic therapy (PDT) using CQDs, as in the study reported by Choppadandi et al. [513]. This study used CQDs conjugated with iron oxide nanoparticles (M-CQDs) for the in vivo treatment of mice. Fig. 21b shows a photograph of an untreated tumor-bearing nude mouse used as a control sample. In Fig. 21c–a mouse treated with M-CQDs is presented, while Fig. 21d shows a mouse treated with the CQD-metal nanoparticle complex, which was subjected to hyperthermia and photodynamic therapy. In all cases, the photographs were taken 15 days after the start of the treatment. The results indicate that even without irradiation or hyperthermic treatment, there is an enhanced therapeutic effect when M-CQDs are used. The authors suggest that the mechanism of action is strongly related to the release of reactive oxygen species catalyzed by M-CQDs during photodynamic therapy. Although tumor growth inhibition was observed when M-CQDs were injected without hyperthermic treatment or photodynamic therapy, the tumor was completely eradicated when the combined treatment was applied. The authors report no obvious toxic effects from the treatment, nor were there any side effects observed in organs such as the spleen, liver, heart, lungs, or kidneys. These findings highlight the significant potential for clinical applications in cancer treatment soon.

CQDs can also monitor the drug delivery process in real time, track the therapeutic response, and provide feedback for personalized treatment plans [514]. Hassan et al. [515] synthesized nitrogen-CQDs from malic acid and EDTA to monitor the drug release process. Ibuprofen was taken as a model drug using encapsulation with sodium alginate. The release could be tracked using spectrofluorometry and UV–vis spectroscopy, showing a release of 42 % after 24h at 40 °C or higher, the regular temperature for an illness state. The release medium's pH was also studied to conclude that the best place to deliver the ibuprofen was the duodenum instead of the stomach. This study with Ibu-CQD capsules contributes to drug release and bioimaging.

Research also explores using CQDs in photodynamic therapy (PDT), which generate reactive oxygen species upon light activation to kill cancer cells [516]. This approach can be integrated with imaging capabilities for precise localization and treatment of tumors [517]. CQDs' versatility, biocompatibility, and adjustable properties make them potent nanomedicine tools. They have the potential to revolutionize drug delivery systems, improve diagnostic accuracy, and enhance therapeutic interventions, ultimately leading to better healthcare outcomes [518].

A special interest has emerged in a particular type of CQDs for biomedical applications, known as second near-infrared (NIR-II) carbon dots. These nanomaterials exhibit significantly enhanced absorption and emission ranges, particularly between 1000 and 1700 nm [281]. Unlike the first near-infrared region (750–1000 nm), CQDs emitting in the NIR-II window have attracted considerable attention due to their superior tissue penetration depth and improved signal-to-noise ratio compared to emissions in the visible region [519]. This has enabled the acquisition of even higher-quality in vivo imaging, facilitating cancer diagnosis and treatment [246].The synthesis of this type of CQDs generally follows traditional top-down and bottom-up approaches [520,521]; however, it typically requires harsher reaction conditions, such as the use of strong acids or alkalis [522]. As a result, the CQDs produced often exhibit low QYs, highlighting the need of highly efficient synthesis methodologies for their production. Moreover, efficient purification steps are crucial for obtaining high-quality NIR-II emissions [523]. Additional strategies, such as surface modifications [244], elemental doping [524], and hybridization with other nanoparticles or nanomaterials [525] have also been explored to enhance the functionality and performance of NIR-II CQDs. The applications in nanomedicine have demonstrated remarkable performance, for example, in bioimaging [526]. Beyond mere morphological description, their enhanced penetration capabilities have enabled, for instance, the visualization of the gastrointestinal tract and the monitoring of its pH changes [527]. Furthermore, differential staining for biomedical purposes has been evaluated, for example, in the differentiation between normal and antibiotic-resistant bacteria [528]. Approaches such as photodynamic therapy have also been explored with this type of CQD. Han et al. [519] illustrate the use of lasers and NIR-II CQDs for tumor heating and the promotion of apoptosis in cancerous cells, with minimal damage to healthy tissues, proposing a non-invasive therapy. Additionally, approaches in which these CQDs can mimic enzymes to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) within the tumor, leading to its death through oxidative stress mechanisms, are among the most prominent studies of this CQD [529]. However, despite the important results related to the NIR-II CQDs, significant challenges remain regarding the control of synthesis, standardization of purification processes, increasing the QY of samples, and ensuring biocompatibility and long-term toxicity evaluation.

Due to their high surface area and ease of functionalization, CQDs have been studied for their potential to improve the efficiency of chemical flooding [530,531], enhance the viscosity of polymer solutions [532] or act as surfactants [533], thereby improving the displacement efficiency of oil from the reservoir. Afzalitabar et al. [534] synthesized MoS2 QDs/N-doped CQDs nanohybrid via a hydrothermal method to improve oil recovery with very low nanofluid concentration, reducing interfacial tension and enhance oil recovery due to changing the wettability of reservoir rock. Additionally, the fluorescence properties of CQDs can be exploited for in-situ monitoring of the EOR process, enabling real-time tracking of the chemical agents within the reservoir [535]. This capability is particularly advantageous in optimizing recovery strategies and maximizing the yield from mature fields.

CQDs also play a crucial role in reservoir characterization [536], a process essential for accurately assessing a reservoir's properties. The unique optical properties of CQDs, such as their tunable fluorescence, make them ideal candidates for use as tracers in reservoir studies [537]. These tracers can be introduced into the reservoir to monitor fluid flow paths, permeability, and other critical reservoir characteristics [45]. Franco et al. [265] synthesized CQDs from Mortiño extract using microwave-assisted carbonization, evaluating the CQDs under reservoir conditions. The CQDs were analyzed in 10 producer wells, showing that they are helpful as inter-well tracers and the different interconnections between the different wells in the field were established. As shown in the right side of Fig. 22 a, the tracer appeared 3 days after the injection at a concentration of 3.1 mg L−1, increasing with time to a maximum concentration of 41.3 mg L−1 and declining after 25 days. On the left side, the tracer appeared on the third day at a concentration of 4.2 mg L−1, increasing to a maximum of 15.4 mg L−1 after 3 days and declining 8 days later. Another study for assessing inter-well connectivity was presented by Rosales et al. [54]. The researchers synthesized CQDs from orange peel as agro-industrial waste using a hydrothermal protocol. After developing the nanomaterials under laboratory conditions, the prepared CQDs, and other commercials were injected into a field where monitoring the effluents allowed the determination of connectivity of five first-line producer wells, as shown in Fig. 22b. In this case, the detection of the green tracer appeared in the fourth month after the injection and was detected for 3 months, and the blue tracer appeared a month after the injection and was detected for six months. This information was consistent with the distance between the production wells and enabled the researchers to describe the interconnection graphically. It is important to mention the appearance of green and blue CQDs in wells P5 and P6, showing no interference in its fluorescence signal. Using CQDs as tracers offers advantages over traditional methods due to their stability, non-toxicity, and ability to provide high-resolution data.

CQDs have also been explored as additives in drilling fluids in drilling operations to enhance their performance [538]. The incorporation of CQDs into drilling fluids can improve their thermal stability, rheological properties, and lubrication, thereby reducing the risks associated with wellbore instability and stuck pipe incidents [539]. Franco et al. [540] developed and incorporated CQDs in drilling fluids to characterize them and identify the contribution of different production wells in the same reservoir. Moreover, CQDs can serve as efficient plugging agents in drilling fluids, helping to mitigate the loss of drilling fluids in porous formations, a common issue in petroleum drilling operations.

Corrosion of equipment and pipelines is another major concern in the petroleum industry, leading to significant maintenance costs and operational downtime. CQDs have been investigated as corrosion inhibitors due to their ability to form protective layers on metal surfaces [541]. Their small size allows them to penetrate and adhere to microscopic imperfections on the metal surface, providing a more uniform and effective barrier against corrosive agents. Mazo et al. [542] developed and evaluated a nanomaterial composed of CQDs to reduce the corrosion rate on carbon steel surfaces exposed to corrosive media, such as production brine and strong acid solutions used in oil and gas operations. This application not only extends the life of petroleum infrastructure but also reduces the frequency of maintenance and the associated costs.

In general, the integration of the CQDs holds significant importance in impacting various aspects of the oil and gas industry [543,544] as mentioned before, such as drilling [545], hydrates, and corrosion inhibition [546,547], well tracers [265], water and polymer flooding [264], foam stability and enhanced oil recovery [258], among others. Fig. 23 represents some of the applications reported in the literature using CQDs in the oil and gas industry, where it can be noted a higher interest in well tracers and enhanced oil recovery applications, as well as research into new applications for the industry, such as the removal of oil films and changes in wettability of the medium. This is due to the multifaceted characteristics associated with optical properties, thermal stability, environmental friendliness and possible functionalization of CQDs.

Cancer remains the second leading cause of death globally, with millions of lives lost each year [548]. Effective treatment hinges not only on early diagnosis but also on targeted therapeutic approaches that minimize systemic toxicity. Conventional cancer therapies such as chemotherapy and radiotherapy suffer from non-specific biodistribution, low bioavailability, and pronounced side effects due to their broad activity spectrum. Nanotechnology, particularly carbon-based nanomaterials, is reshaping oncological strategies by overcoming many of these limitations. Among these, CQDs have garnered increasing attention for their photophysical properties, biocompatibility, surface tunability, and capacity for multi-modal therapeutic integration [549].

Advanced nanomaterials, including CQDs, are aiding in the resolution of complex challenges in oncology—such as tumor hypoxia, heterogeneous tumor microenvironments, poor drug stability, limited tissue penetration, rapid drug clearance, and lack of tumor specificity—while enhancing therapeutic efficacy [549]. Compared to other carbon-based systems like fullerenes and nanotubes, CQDs offer several advantages including ease of synthesis, aqueous dispersibility, low cytotoxicity, and high quantum yield, which position them as ideal candidates for applications in phototherapy, drug delivery, and diagnostics.

Photothermal therapy (PTT) is one of the most promising applications of CQDs in oncology. In this technique, agents that absorb specific light wavelengths (especially in the near-infrared (NIR) range) convert light energy into localized heat, inducing tumor cell apoptosis with minimal damage to surrounding healthy tissues [550]. CQDs possess abundant π-electron systems that mimic free electrons in metal nanoparticles, enabling efficient photothermal conversion. For instance, Zheng et al. [118] synthesized CyCDs using cyanine dye (CyOH) and polyethylene glycol (PEG800), achieving a strong NIR emission peak at 820 nm and a photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of 38.7 %. These CyCDs exhibited excellent tumor-targeting capabilities and were successfully used for both NIR fluorescence imaging and photothermal ablation of tumors. Similarly, Bao et al. [551,552] developed NIR-emissive CQDs via a hydrothermal method using sulfur- and nitrogen-rich precursors. These NIR CDs showed an impressive photothermal conversion efficiency of 59 % under 808 nm laser irradiation. Their fluorescence and photoacoustic properties enabled in vivo tumor visualization and therapy monitoring. Notably, the CDs accumulated passively in tumor tissues and were cleared renally, thereby reducing long-term systemic toxicity. Lan et al. [553] further extended the applicability of CQDs by synthesizing sulfur and selenium co-doped CDs from polythiophene and diphenyl diselenide, which demonstrated substantial two-photon absorption and 58.2 % photothermal efficiency—an approach advantageous for deeper tissue penetration through two-photon excitation. In another study, Li et al. [126] designed folic acid-functionalized CDs (FA-CDs) loaded with the NIR dye IR780. These complexes, with a photothermal conversion efficiency of 87.9 %, effectively targeted tumor cells and induced their ablation under NIR irradiation.

In addition to photothermal effects, CQDs also serve as efficient platforms for photodynamic therapy (PDT), where light-activated photosensitizers generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) to induce cancer cell death. PDT offers high spatiotemporal precision, but its success is limited by the poor stability and targeting efficiency of conventional photosensitizers, as well as their dependency on tumor oxygenation levels [550]. To address these challenges, Chen et al. [554] synthesized nanoscale covalent organic frameworks (CCOF-1 and CCOF-2) using aldehyde-functionalized CQDs and BODIPY derivatives, which were further PEGylated for enhanced aqueous stability, as shown Fig. 24 . The resulting CCOF-2@PEG displayed excellent biocompatibility and ROS generation efficiency, proving highly effective in vitro and in vivo for tumor inhibition.

Additional innovations include porphyrin-functionalized CQDs, which show intrinsic photosensitizing capabilities. In a study by Li et al. [555], these porphyrin-CDs exhibited high photostability, efficient cellular uptake, and notable cytotoxic effects upon irradiation. Moreover, CQDs synthesized from ginger juice by Li et al. [556] demonstrated selective cytotoxicity against human hepatocellular carcinoma (HepG2) cells while sparing normal cells such as MCF-10A and FL83B. These CQDs, averaging 4.3 ± 0.8 nm in diameter, induced apoptosis in HepG2 cells through activation of p53 signaling and ROS generation, suggesting an intrinsic therapeutic effect.

The versatility of CQDs also extends to multimodal and synergistic treatments, integrating PTT, PDT, and targeted chemotherapy in a single nanoplatform. Their surface functionalization allows for conjugation with targeting ligands, therapeutic agents, and imaging molecules, enabling site-specific accumulation and spatiotemporal control over therapy. Moreover, their photoluminescence supports real-time bioimaging for diagnosis and therapy monitoring, aligning with the emerging paradigm of theranostics.

The emergence of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has opened up sustainable and innovative pathways for applications in agriculture, particularly as nanofertilizers and plant growth promoters. An et al. [557] demonstrated that CQDs synthesized via high-temperature carbonization of peanut bran could significantly improve the fresh weight (FW) of lettuce by up to 25 % compared to untreated controls, with optimal effects observed at specific concentrations. Similarly, Agnol et al. [558] reported that CQDs derived from thermal lysis of Spirulina sp. enhanced lentil seed germination and growth at concentrations of 0.05–0.1 mg mL−1. Liang et al. [559] further expanded this scope by utilizing plastic waste as a carbon source to produce CQDs capable of improving root hydration, biomass accumulation, and seed germination in pea plants, highlighting their potential in circular economy frameworks.

The ability of CQDs to enhance photosynthetic processes is a particularly valuable feature. Wang et al. [560] prepared CQDs from natural xylose with varied sp2 carbon domain structures, which promoted biotic-abiotic interactions at the chloroplast interface, improving electron transport and light harvesting. In another study, Cheng et al. [561] reported that gram-scale biomass-derived CQDs enhanced multiple photosynthetic metrics, including chlorophyll synthesis (up to 100 %), ferredoxin activity (40–80 %), Rubisco activation (20–110 %), gene upregulation (20–70 %), and net photosynthetic rate (up to 300 %), offering comprehensive insight into their physiological mechanisms.

Apart from growth stimulation, biomass-based CQDs also contribute to plant protection and nutritional enhancement. Gonzalez et al. [562] developed CQDs from agave bagasse that emitted UV-blue fluorescence, providing UV shielding and improving seed germination and growth rates. Li et al. [563] synthesized selenium-doped CQDs (Se-CQDs) from algal biomass that, when applied as a foliar spray, improved the biological mass content (BMC), fruit quality, and selenium uptake in tomato plants. Interestingly, their efficacy was shown to be dependent on the plant's phenological stage, with optimal benefits during flowering and fruiting periods—emphasizing the importance of timing in agricultural applications.

Furthermore, Sankaranarayanan et al. [560] synthesized CQDs from bio-oil extracted from brown algae, which facilitated mung bean seed germination and accelerated vegetative growth as shown Fig. 25 . These findings collectively suggest that biomass-based CQDs not only serve as nutrient sources but also act as growth regulators, photosynthesis enhancers, and protective agents against abiotic stressors.

Biomass-based CQDs, due to their low cost, high functionality, and environmentally benign synthesis routes, stand out as a new class of smart agricultural nanomaterials. They offer a dual advantage of valorizing biomass waste and addressing global food security challenges through yield improvement and stress resistance. Their integration into nanofertilizer formulations marks a significant step toward more sustainable, efficient, and resilient agricultural practices.

The term "antibacterial activity" can encompass various modes of interaction with microbial cells, ranging from bacteriostatic effects—such as the inhibition of cellular proliferation—to complete bactericidal actions where bacteria are irreversibly inactivated [564]. While the inhibition of bacterial growth primarily aims to delay replication and support the host immune system in clearing infections [565], bactericidal activity involves irreversible damage that halts metabolism and reproduction altogether [566]. Both outcomes can be associated with structural damage to the bacterial envelope [567].

Among the various nanomaterials explored for antimicrobial purposes, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained prominence due to their tunable surface chemistry, photoluminescent properties, and efficient reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. The antibacterial efficacy of CQDs is largely attributed to oxidative stress mechanisms mediated by ROS such as superoxide anions, hydroxyl radicals, and hydrogen peroxide [568]. These species, once produced near bacterial surfaces, attack essential biomolecules—lipids, proteins, and DNA—causing membrane disruption and cell death when antioxidant defenses are overwhelmed [569].

Surface functionalization of CQDs with heteroatoms like nitrogen enhances their ability to generate ROS by introducing electronic defects that facilitate charge transfer processes [569]. Additionally, nitrogen doping tends to impart a positive surface charge to the nanodots, which promotes electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged bacterial membranes and assists in localized ROS delivery [569]. The short-lived nature of ROS—on the order of microseconds for most species and milliseconds for H2O2—requires that their generation occur in proximity to bacterial targets [570].

Apart from ROS-mediated pathways, CQDs with inherent cationic properties also exhibit direct antibacterial activity. Materials synthesized from spermidine [566] or quaternary ammonium salts [571] have demonstrated strong binding affinity to bacterial cell wall components such as peptidoglycan and membrane proteins, impeding cell wall synthesis across both Gram-positive and Gram-negative strains—including resistant phenotypes and persister cells. These functionalized CQDs display minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) that are drastically lower—by up to 250,000-fold in some cases—than the original precursor molecules. For context, MICs for standard antibiotics like gentamicin or rifampicin range from 4 to >64 μg mL−1, depending on the pathogen and resistance profile [572].

Moreover, CQDs derived from Artemisia argyi selectively target Gram-negative bacteria by interfering with enzymatic pathways exclusive to their outer membrane synthesis [573]. Once the integrity of the bacterial wall is compromised, CQDs can further penetrate and interact with intracellular components, including nucleic acids, intensifying their cytotoxic effects.

An additional mechanism involves the retention of pharmacologically active moieties during the synthesis process. For instance, CQDs synthesized via low-temperature carbonization (150 °C) of gentamicin sulfate retain antibacterial functionalities comparable to the parent drug, with MICs similar to those of gentamicin against S. aureus and E. coli [567]. However, excessive carbonization (>190–200 °C) may degrade these functional groups, reducing activity. Interestingly, CQDs carbonized at 180 °C demonstrated enhanced activity under acidic conditions, such as those found in bacterial biofilms [567].

CQDs also show promise in biofilm management. Bacterial biofilms—aggregates embedded within a protective extracellular matrix—are notoriously resistant to both immune clearance and antibiotics [574]. CQDs capable of disrupting this extracellular polymeric substance (EPS) layer contribute to "biofilm dispersal," rendering bacteria more vulnerable to therapeutic agents [575]. For example, CQDs derived from dimethyloctadecyl-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl] ammonium chloride not only broke down established S. aureus biofilms but also exhibited bactericidal activity at relatively high concentrations (∼1000 μg/mL) [576]. Other formulations, such as citric acid/polyethyleneimine CQDs modified with 2,3-dimethylmaleic anhydride, selectively disrupted biofilms without killing the bacterial cells, even at similar doses, as shown in Fig. 26 [575]. The Figure depicts cross-sectional images of non-EPS producing S. epidermidis ATCC 12228 biofilms after 4 h exposure to buffer, 125 μg/mL C-dots without DMMA or CDMMA-dots in 10 mM phosphate buffer at pH 7.4, demonstrating their excellent performance.

Beyond efficacy, an important consideration is the potential of CQDs to circumvent bacterial resistance mechanisms. Unlike traditional antibiotics that target specific biosynthetic pathways—often leading to rapid emergence of resistance—CQDs exert multifaceted, non-specific stress responses such as membrane perturbation and ROS induction [569]. For instance, nitrogen-doped CQDs synthesized from bis-quaternary ammonium salts not only exhibited lower MICs than several antibiotics, but also provoked significantly less resistance development in MRSA strains compared to penicillin [571]. This reduced resistance risk is a particularly valuable trait, as many contemporary antibiotics lose clinical effectiveness shortly after introduction [97,98]. Nevertheless, it has been reported that some bacteria might upregulate antioxidant enzymes in response to CQD-induced oxidative stress, raising questions about potential long-term adaptation [577].

Many research and development efforts are underway in different application areas, each progressing towards designing increasingly efficient materials through structural modifications that enable specific applications. Developing functionalized or passivated CQDs tailored to specific applications is a rapidly growing field. However, large-scale production remains the primary challenge in applying these materials. Although several companies already include CQDs in their product portfolios, the available quantities are limited: typically, 100 mg of pure product or 10 mL at a concentration of 2 mg mL−1, priced at 800 euros and 348 euros, respectively. Industrial applications, such as tracers in the oil and gas industry, require much larger quantities. Estimates suggest that around 8.1 kg of CQDs would be needed for a pilot test, costing between 64 and 140 million euros at current commercial prices. Similarly, applications like photocatalysis for treating large volumes of water (1 million liters, at an average concentration of 0.5 g L−1) would require approximately 500 kg of pure CQDs, with costs rising to at least 4 billion euros. This makes the use of CQDs at such scales financially prohibitive. Therefore, the real challenge lies in achieving large-scale production and significantly reducing costs during scaling, so that these applications, which show great promise at the laboratory level, can become viable for industrial use. No simple route has yet been designed for mass production, especially for applications using agro-industrial residues, which presents reproducibility challenges due to these raw materials' inherent limitations. Technological advances are needed to establish an appropriate pathway for mass production without compromising product functionality and minimizing environmental impact.

Despite significant progress in developing applications that exploit the optoelectronic properties of CQDs, many areas are still evolving. Advancing these technologies to a more mature stage requires substantial research efforts. Among the most innovative applications under development is the selective staining of cellular structures in sperm cells, which could offer a cost-effective alternative to current expensive fluorescent probes. Additionally, using CQDs for CO2 storage and its conversion into valuable products, such as fuels or chemicals via photocatalytic processes, presents a promising approach to reducing greenhouse gas emissions. Another potential application is in soil remediation, where CQDs could facilitate the immobilization or degradation of heavy metals or persistent organic compounds, enhancing soil quality. Furthermore, clean energy production, such as hydrogen generation through photocatalysis, could benefit from CQDs, positioning them as critical components in sustainable energy solutions. CQDs may also have a role in bioremediation processes.

It is important to carefully consider the type of quantum dots used for these emerging applications. While many developments have leveraged agro-industrial residues, it is essential to complement these advances with comprehensive life cycle analyses. Simply asserting that these processes are more sustainable because they use waste materials is insufficient. A thorough life cycle assessment, including energy requirements for carbonization, purification, raw material inputs, and waste generation, is necessary. Another challenge for applications using agro-industrial by-products is the issue of product standardization. QY results often vary significantly, even when using the same synthesis method. A considerable gap remains in understanding the key synthesis parameters required to consistently produce CQDs with competitive QYs.

Although the hydrothermal method is most reported in the literature as a low-cost and efficient technique, the scalability of this method and its advantages over other routes are not entirely clear. Studies comparing the conversion efficiency and the product obtained from different synthesis routes would help clarify this. In environmental terms, future studies should focus on maximizing the conversion of by-products into quantum dots, which would reduce raw material loss and improve the overall efficiency of the process, making it more eco-friendly.

While most CQDs achieve significant QYs with emissions in the blue and green ranges, those emitting at longer wavelengths, such as red or near-infrared, exhibit much lower QYs and broader absorption spectra. The availability of a wide range of emission wavelengths remains a challenge. In fact, among the CQDs used in the various applications discussed in this review, approximately 64 % emit fluorescence in the green or blue range (476–570 nm), only 6 % in the yellow range (571–581 nm), and the remaining 30 % in the orange-red range (581–780 nm). A common challenge for CQDs emitting at longer wavelengths is their low QY and instability over time. Thus, obtaining CQDs with broader absorption bands and comparable QYs remains highly desirable for photocatalysis and detection applications. Achieving this requires a deeper understanding of CQD photostability and developing new synthesis techniques to improve fluorescence QYs under these conditions, thereby expanding the spectrum of applications.

One significant limitation is that most of these developments occur in controlled laboratory environments. Bringing CQD-based cancer treatments to more complex organisms is also a challenge. Although the results are promising for cancer therapy, current studies are still limited to animal models, which, although representative, require a higher level of sophistication before moving to clinical trials. Only through such trials can the effectiveness of these systems be verified and eventually applied to benefit human health. In the coming years, it will be crucial to integrate these technologies with devices and assays that can be tested in situ, in vivo, in the field, or for real-time monitoring. Nonetheless, economic, environmental, and technical feasibility studies are necessary to ensure effective implementation.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have rapidly emerged as a key nanomaterial with extensive potential across various fields, primarily due to their unique optical properties, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and versatile synthesis methods. CQDs stand out among quantum materials because of their ability to be synthesized from a wide range of carbon-rich precursors through both top-down and bottom-up approaches, which allows for cost-effective and environmentally friendly production. The burgeoning research into CQDs has explored their applications across several domains, including bioimaging, biosensing, photocatalysis, and environmental monitoring, which positions CQDs as a central player in both industrial and biomedical advancements.

The synthesis techniques for CQDs have evolved significantly, with methods such as hydrothermal, microwave, electrochemical, and combustion-based approaches offering scalable solutions for producing high-quality CQDs with tunable properties. One of the major advancements in recent years has been the ability to fine-tune the size, shape, and surface functionalization of CQDs, which is critical for optimizing their optical and electronic behaviors. Surface passivation and doping with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus have further expanded the range of optical properties, enabling CQDs to exhibit enhanced photoluminescence and improved QY.

However, challenges remain in achieving uniformity in particle size and controlling emission characteristics with high precision. The diversity of synthesis methods, while beneficial in providing multiple routes to obtain CQDs, has also introduced variability in the consistency of the products. Future research should aim to standardize the synthesis protocols and develop more refined techniques for controlling the structural and optical properties of CQDs at the molecular level. Achieving such control would be particularly beneficial in applications where precise optical properties are required, such as in bioimaging and optoelectronic devices.

CQDs have demonstrated a wide array of applications, particularly in fields that demand high-performance nanomaterials. In bioimaging, CQDs have proven to be excellent fluorescent markers, offering high-resolution imaging of cells and tissues due to their strong luminescent properties and biocompatibility. Their low toxicity makes them especially suitable for in vivo applications, where long-term safety is a major concern. CQDs have also been employed in biosensing applications to detect specific ions, biomolecules, and other analytes with high sensitivity and selectivity. Their ability to quench fluorescence in the presence of target molecules has enabled the development of highly sensitive detection platforms for medical diagnostics and environmental monitoring.

In the environmental sector, CQDs have been explored for their catalytic properties, particularly in photocatalysis and electrocatalysis. For instance, CQD-based photocatalysts have shown promise in degrading pollutants such as organic dyes and heavy metals in wastewater. By enhancing the separation of charge carriers and improving light absorption, CQDs significantly boost the efficiency of photocatalytic processes, making them valuable in environmental remediation. Additionally, CQDs' incorporation into membranes for water purification technologies has improved permeability, selectivity, and resistance to fouling, presenting a viable solution for addressing global water scarcity issues.

The oil and gas industry has also benefited from the unique properties of CQDs. These nanomaterials have been successfully integrated into drilling fluids to enhance rheological properties and reduce formation damage. Furthermore, CQDs have been used as tracers in inter-well monitoring, providing valuable insights into the behavior of fluids within geological formations.

Despite the significant strides made in CQD research, several challenges remain that must be addressed to unlock their full potential. One of the most pressing issues is the scalability of CQD production. While laboratory-scale synthesis methods have yielded promising results, translating these processes into industrial-scale production without compromising the quality of the CQDs is a significant challenge. Additionally, there is a need for more thorough investigation into the long-term stability of CQDs, especially when used in medical, biological and environmental applications. Understanding how these nanomaterials behave over extended periods, particularly in terms of degradation and potential toxicity, will be critical for ensuring their safe and sustainable use.

The environmental impact of CQD synthesis and disposal is another area that requires attention. While CQDs are often touted as environmentally friendly due to their carbon-based composition, the use of harsh chemicals in some synthesis methods could pose environmental risks. Therefore, developing greener and more sustainable synthesis routes, such as those involving biomass or waste materials, will be essential for minimizing the ecological footprint of CQD production.

In terms of applications, further research is needed to enhance the performance of CQD-based sensors, particularly in complex real-world environments. While CQDs have shown exceptional sensitivity in detecting heavy metals and other analytes in controlled laboratory conditions, their performance in more challenging matrices, such as polluted water or biological fluids, needs improvement. Enhancing the selectivity of CQD-based sensors, as well as improving their robustness against interference from other substances, will be key to their widespread adoption in environmental monitoring and healthcare diagnostics.

Another promising area for future research is the exploration of CQDs in emerging technologies, such as energy conversion and storage. The ability of CQDs to absorb light over a wide spectrum makes them ideal candidates for use in solar cells and other photovoltaic devices. Moreover, their electronic properties could be leveraged in the development of next-generation batteries and supercapacitors, where efficient charge storage and transfer are critical. Exploring these applications will require a deeper understanding of the fundamental electronic and photophysical properties of CQDs, as well as innovative approaches to integrating them into device architectures.
